My personal bias comes about due to the fact that I have a project called [Metrics](https://gitlab.com/tmose1106/metrics) which calculates the SLOC, number of comments, etc within a file or project, and it determines the type of a file based upon its file extension. Nevertheless, this is your project, and I respect your opinion on the subject.

Finally, would you like me to handle your two requests in a new commit?

Maybe I'm misunderstanding but are you saying that space directions and measurement frames should not have none in them?

Based on the NRRD specs, it shows that none is possible for rows of the matrices. When testing, I used NRRDs command line interface to generate some NRRDs to test pynrrd against.

Or, maybe you're saying we should derive the two fields based on the kinds field? I had that thought but I was slightly concerned kinds may not be present in all NRRDs.

My general thought process was that requiring parse_optional_matrix to be floating point does no harm. If the floating numbers are whole numbers, then they will print out as integers with no decimal points.

Do you have any reason for why the dtype argument needs to be present in the function?
Thanks!

Indeed, this tries to accomplish several goals:

* DRY improvement through the extraction of common code paths.
* Improve the scenario where transformation operations occur multiple times within a single process by using a persistent interpreter instance, where possible.
* Document and automate the process of gathering NPM dependencies to reduce the impact of things like #6 (allowing users to update it themselves more easily).

I agree that having two paths for some things like Babel is bad; I hadn't yet tackled the simpler functional interface.  However, I see single use as a specialization of multiple use as it's simpler to instantiate, invoke, then clean up, than fiddle with optional arguments for persistence.

I'll meditate further on the problem.  The "right" solution will very likely be in the middle-ground: having a class based general interface for efficient single-process reuse, with subclasses for each particular filter, with the WebAssets-specific glue as mixin?

The way the current WebAsset Filter subclass lazily constructs the interpreter, one could simply construct pre-made global singleton instances to use as the functional interface.  (Just need to write a `__call__` method, there.)  Ideas, ideas!  :D

There is one final issue related to this whole PR/branch.  I've been attempting to integrate [`rollup`](http://rollupjs.org), but unfortunately it only offers an asynchronous (promise-based) interface.  Even after extreme effort (digging up every Promise mocking library I could find) I have been unsuccessful in getting results back out. I'll push that incomplete filter implementation to illustrate.  My end goal is to completely remove any and all non-Python dependency, e.g. avoiding grunt, webpack, or other Node bundling tools.  I have a WebAssets configuration like the following:

```python
from web.ext.assets import Bundle
from dukpy.webassets import RollupJS

				'illico.rita.js:lib/medium-editor-markdown/dist/me-markdown.standalone.js',  # markdown output support
				'illico.rita.js:lib/medium-editor-list/build/medium-editor-list.js',  # automatic list creation
				
				output = 'third-party-%(version)s.js',

			),

						'javascripthon',  # input filter

> This is by design. If an integer is fed, without additional context the
> program cannot know how many bytes you expect it to process, only how many are
> non-zero in the integer.

Right.

```python
>>> b58encode_int(0)
b'1'
>>> b58encode(bytes(bytearray.fromhex('00' * 16)))
b'1111111111111111'
```

> The base58 spec says that 1 prefixes are ignorables.

Cool. So prefix `1`s are throwaway and are thusly dropped, resulting in shorter
encoder output.

`gladLoadGLLoader` is just a function that takes a pointer to a `GLADloadproc` function. This function (of typedef `GLADloadproc`), takes a string and uses one of the functions available in the OS to return a pointer to the OpenGL implementation function that has the same name in that string. You can see all that in the generated `glad.c`.

I doubt this is something Curio should solve.
At least never be eager for read().

If any length is supplied (i.e read(10000))
Curio 'might' be eager if it would not block.
But I'm not sure..

So I'm at the side of non-eagerness.
I consider eagerness as premature optimization
for curio.

Sufficient 'eager' user might side step to a
pure blocking sync thread which is also
possible with Curio.

Please keep Curio stupid. This way we can be sure
what to expect.

We definitely need some way to send work into the kernel from other
contexts -- including other threads -- that doesn't involve busy-polling.
(Busy polling is fine for the tkinter case since it requires a busy polling
loop anyway, but it's not a general solution.) Maybe
run_in_thread(threading.Queue.get) is that way, though, so curio.Queue
doesn't need to be involved? Though it would be good to have a primitive
that didn't require spending whole threads on just waiting around.

On Nov 19, 2016 09:18, "David Beazley" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

It seems that using queues are also a technique for communicating between
Tkinter and threads.

In addressing this, I've been doing a lot of thinking about the Curio Queue
and Event objects. Right now there are synchronous put() and set() methods.
However, maybe they are completely unneeded--actions that need to be
awaited can be queued up in a deque and processed later. This is much more
general purpose and flexible. So, I would encourage you to use that
technique instead of the Queue object. I'm probably going to remove the
synchronous implementation of those methods (which were always highly
experimental and not really documented).

Looking at the code for the chunked transfer as well as the non-chunked upload, it seems we could pull these out into a generator that accepts the environ and yields either one chunk at a time, or one `DEFAULT_BLOCK_SIZE` at a time.

The default handlePUT could then loop over this and call `fileobj.write` as it does now. 

If the request is not chunked, we'd still prefer to pass `environ["wsgi.input"]` directly to our networking library, but if it is chunked, we can use the generator to form a readable object to pass on.

The k-prototypes implementation in this package is heavily based on the (pretty optimized) k-modes implementation. My focus was initially more focused on readability than optimization, so I'm sure there is room for improvement on the k-means side of k-prototypes.

The scikit-learn version of k-means is likely highly optimized. For example, I see that the squared norms of data points are pre-computed. I have no intentions currently to implement further speed optimizations for k-prototypes, but PRs are of course welcome.

Theoretically, I see no reason why k-prototypes would be much slower than k-means. There might be some minor overhead because you have to do both k-modes and k-means updates, but of course you only do the former on the categorical and the latter on the numerical features so the computational complexity wouldn't change.

Anyway, this isn't a dlib problem.  You just have to read the manual carefully for the pyhton multi-processing features you are using and understand their limitations and how to use them.

> And if i want to send another request immediately after i've runned Dispatcher, i have to run multiple Requesters in different threads and to have common queue ?

No-no-no! That would be a weird mix of asynchronous and threaded models (which would work though).

I think we need to define "immediately"...

Generally, the whole thing is event-driven. So you need an event to trigger your request scheduling code. The event could be for example a reception of a response (or timeout). Or if there is no specific event to piggyback on, you can register your own callable with transport dispatcher so you get your code run every once in a while where you could decide is it time to schedule another query or not yet. Once you return from your timer function, the control is back to the hands of the main loop.

Ah, you can interleave these build/wait phases, the main thing is that you should only drop into the waiting state after you are done all the more urgent stuff, because waiting might take time (up to a timeout or a timer tick).

```Python
import bt
import pandas as pd

class RunOnDataFrameDates(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self, dates):
        self.dates = pd.to_datetime(dates)

    def __call__(self, target):
        return target.now in self.dates

class OrderedWeights(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self, weights):
        self.target_weights = weights

    def __call__(self, target):
        target.temp['weights'] = dict(zip(target.temp['selected'], self.target_weights))
        return True
        
def getStrategySet(date_list, numbuckets, nummonths, stratname):
    strat_list = []
    for x in range(0, numbuckets):
        momname = stratname + 'mom' + str(x)
        mom0 = bt.Strategy(momname, [RunOnDataFrameDates(date_list[x::numbuckets]),
                        bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                        bt.algos.SelectMomentum(2,pd.DateOffset(months=nummonths)),
                        OrderedWeights([0.7, 0.3]),
                        bt.algos.Rebalance()])
        strat_list.append(mom0)
    return strat_list

def momentum_algo(data, date_mm, numbuckets,nummonths, name):
    strat_list = getStrategySet(date_mm, numbuckets,nummonths,name)
    s = bt.Strategy(name, [bt.algos.RunMonthly(),
                           bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                           bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                           bt.algos.Rebalance()],
                    strat_list)
    return bt.Backtest(s, data)

mom4 = momentum_algo(data,date_mm,4,10, '4buckets')

res = bt.run(mom2,mom3,mom4)

res.display()

Seems like you have an old JSON file from TinyDB 1.x. In version 2.0 (back in 2014) we switched to the dict-based storage scheme, see #13 and #14 for details. In short, having the dict-based scheme allows for quick lookup by document ID without having to scan the entire list of values.

> Is it possible to configure TinyDB to use an array ?

That's not possible and I don't think there are many advantages to going back to a list-based scheme (or maintaining two types of storage schemes for that matter).

> That's one issue. Another issue is that we quickly run out of color/texture-based styles in large vocabularies, so if you're relying solely on rectangle style (and not vertical position) to differentiate, it's easy to get lost. We can chain up property cyclers to move from flat to cross-hatched, then to alpha channels, etc, but the returns on this diminish quickly.

It seems to me like a logical and sane way to handle this for chords (where we can have huge, but structured, vocabularies) would be to have color mapped to chord root and vertical position mapped to chord type. The same twelve colors would always correspond to the twelve roots, and for example in a min/maj vocabulary, we'd have two vertical positions.  The number of possible chord types could be inferred at call time or user-specified, and would have a consistent ordering (major on the bottom, then minor, then seventh, etc). Here's an excerpt from my comments on #46:

> The essential change is to allow a TYPEID to be part of a declarator. We introduce two categories of declarator: (1) an id-declarator (equivalent to what is currently just a 'declarator'), and (2) a typeid-declarator, which is identical but uses a TYPEID rather than an ID. In most cases, these two get recombined into a generic 'declarator', which can be either of the two. In the special case of a function definition, its declarator must be an id-declarator (this restriction is valid b/c a function name can't have been typedef'd already because such a typedef would have happened in the same outermost scope).
> 
> However, allowing TYPEIDs in declarators obviously will introduce some parsing conflicts. To fix these, the other main change is to make a restriction to declaration-specifiers (and specifier-qualifier-list) which reflects two things: (1) it must contain at least one type-specifier, and (2) if it contains a typedef-name, it may not contain any other type-specifiers. This is required so that the parser knows when to switch from parsing a specifiers list to parsing a declarator. It's also consistent with declaration-specifiers as described in section 6.7.2.2 of ISO/IEC 9899:TC3.
> 
> After making these changes, you can then remove the existing productions that are workarounds to deal with incorrect parses.

Since I wrote that, there was one other change required, which is to disallow `typeid-declarator`s nested inside parens as part of a parameter declaration. This is because in this situation the TYPEID should always be treated as a `typedef-name`, per section 6.7.5.3.11 of ISO/IEC 9899:TC3:

> If, in a parameter declaration, an identifier can be treated either as a typedef name or as a parameter name, it shall be taken as a typedef name.

I agree it is a big change, and I can provide more tests. I can also add some more detail in the comments if you'd like.

- [X] add `is None` for column
- [X] comment for `_token_coord`

I also modified the regression tests in which the file name was not present to comply with the fixed `assert_coord` api.

If it can inject a driver it will use the driver.

If it cannot inject a driver, it will create a thread and terminate it if it hangs.  The handle that causes NtQueryObject to hang is just ignored and not present in the output for Process Explorer.

I changed the transaction debugging API, so now the error info should be available as `error.debug` dictionary:
```
debug = {'traceback': …,
         'transaction': …,
         'next_stage': …}
```

The `traceback` field is the formatted traceback from the `commit()` try/except statement. The `transaction` is self-clear. The `next_stage` field contains the references to the nested `commit()` stage (like a transaction rollback):

Thanks for the feedback. I do believe Colorama could be greatly enhanced by context manager syntax, as the current syntax using string concatenation feels very unnatural. 

I also implemented another solution to this end, which I feel has the ideal syntactic solution, though its implementation is slightly worse. It would be used like this:

from colorama.contexts import Fore, Back, Style

```python
print('Before any, ', end='')
with Fore.RED:
    print('inside one, ', end='')
    with Back.BLUE:
        print('inside two, ', end='')
        with Fore.WHITE, Style.DIM:
            print('inside three, ', end='')
        print('nested ', end='')
    print('context ', end='')
print('managers.', end='')
```

And in `contexts.py`: 


```python
from colorama import ansi

fore_stack = [ansi.Fore.RESET]
back_stack = [ansi.Back.RESET]
style_stack = [ansi.Style.RESET_ALL]

class Context(object):
    def __init__(self, style, stack):
        self.style = style
        self.stack = stack

    def __enter__(self):
        print(self.style, end='')
        self.stack.append(self.style)

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        if self.style == self.stack[-1]:
            self.stack.pop()
            print(self.stack[-1], end='')

class ForeContexts(object):
    BLACK = Context(ansi.Fore.BLACK, fore_stack)
    RED = Context(ansi.Fore.RED, fore_stack)
    [...]

Fore = ForeContexts()
```

Reading the error:
- **Fatal read error** means something was being read and for some reason reached a state where it no longer could be.
- **SSLProtocol** means it was an SSL connection, i.e. an encrypted connection to the internet.
- **read=polling** means data was being polled, i.e. it was delivered in chunks that the client waited for. This *could* be a HTTP download of a multipart file, but it's more likely that this was the websocket.
- **TimeoutError** means a time limit was set and surpassed without successfully completing the operation.

From this, we can deduce:
- Your bot was connected to the websocket and serving normally.
- Something happened to interrupt the connection to Discord. (This could be anything from Discord having an outage, your router resetting, a faulty cable, or a changeover at your gateway.)
- This interruption lasted long enough that the connection could not be conventionally resumed after it had passed.
- Your bot, no longer having a valid session, closed out with the above error.

Errors of this nature are fairly common on connections without the stability practices you might find on a [VPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtual_private_server). They are not handled automatically on the async branch (0.16.x), so many people use service handlers like [`systemd`](https://www.freedesktop.org/wiki/Software/systemd/) or [`supervisord`](http://supervisord.org/index.html) to automatically restart their bot in these cases.

There is an in-development [`rewrite`](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/tree/rewrite) branch that handles situations like this automatically. If you wish, you can learn more about it on the [discord.py help server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ). Note however that switching to this branch is a large commitment, and may not be suitable for bots that perform important tasks or are developed under commission, for example.

Otherwise, there are some [good](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/7/html/system_administrators_guide/sect-managing_services_with_systemd-unit_files) [online](https://www.raspberrypi-spy.co.uk/2015/10/how-to-autorun-a-python-script-on-boot-using-systemd/) [tutorials](https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-configure-a-linux-service-to-start-automatically-after-a-crash-or-reboot-part-1-practical-examples) on how to use services to keep your bot online.

On Feb 20, 2016 12:27 PM, "Glyph" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Do you think there's a way to support twisted interface without changing
> this core design? My main concern is keeping up w changes to official
> client so that's why I'm hesitant to change the core loop.
>
> Almost.
>
> The big change you have to make is not to adopt Twisted APIs like
> Deferred. The change you need to make is to change the I/O interface from
> one which calls recv to one which *feeds in some data*. The best example
> of this in a *totally* platform-agnostic library is the
> https://github.com/python-hyper/hyper-h2 library from @Lukasa
> <https://github.com/Lukasa>.
>
> Luckily, since recv can always return a short read – and if your code
> isn't dealing with that, it's buggy – there is a simple way to do this with
> your existing code: replace all real socket usage with a fake object whose
> recv raises EAGAIN if no data is available, and returns whatever data is
> available. Then, have an external API which feeds the data in and cranks
> the loop manually.
>
> This does create a situation where calling those APIs directly can lead to
> the EAGAIN exception will bubble up to the caller. But you can address
> this in two ways:
>
>    1. provide two implementations of I/O, one which pulls data in from
>    the main loop, turning the crank on a python-kafka object in Twisted's
>    dataReceived method, for example, or
>    2. use the async implementation of I/O by providing facade objects for
>    use on non-main threads, and use something like Twisted's
>    blockingCallFromThread
>    <https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/15.5.0/api/twisted.internet.threads.html#blockingCallFromThread>
>    to implement Future.get and your kafka subscriber iterator's next
>    (py3: __next__) method.
>
> Does this make sense?
>

> <https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/issues/560#issuecomment-186676385>.

Thanks, but I have to reject this for a couple of reasons

- The reason the build is failing is because the code block that you added isn't valid python syntax. You can only use ``break`` inside of a loop, it doesn't mean anything in the context of ``try...except...``
- ``urllib.request.urlopen`` doesn't exist in python 2, it's called ``urllib.urlopen`` instead. That's one of the reasons it's nice to use ``requests``, because it's compatible across all python versions.
- You can use HTTP **HEAD** instead of **GET** to request the page headers only and follow redirects without loading the whole page.
- I don't like the overhead of making an extra HTTP request every time you load a submission. Because the only time you can submit a shortened url is at start-up (using the **--link** flag), I think it's better for now to just check that link rather than every single submission.

Since it was only a couple of lines, I made an alternate PR that addresses these points. https://github.com/michael-lazar/rtv/pull/276

When you group `players = nflgame.combine(games)` you are aggregating a players entire season stats, destroying any granularity you had in determining his long passes.  What you're doing there is sorting the players that had the most completion yards in a particular season and then displaying their name along with how many total completions they had in that season.

FYI - 2007 is not included in the dataset available to `nflgame`.

Here is how I would attempt to do what you're trying to do.  Please feel free to ask if you have any questions on this.

(58 players completed at least one pass over 30 yards in 2017)
```python
import nflgame
from collections import defaultdict

games = nflgame.games(2017, kind='REG')
plays = nflgame.combine_plays(games).filter(passing_yds=lambda v: v > 30)

long_passers = defaultdict(int)

for play in plays:
    for player in play.players.passing():
        long_passers[player.name] += 1

for name, completions in sorted(long_passers.iteritems(), key=lambda (k,v): (v,k), reverse=True):
    print name, completions
```
```
A.Smith 28
M.Stafford 27
K.Cousins 27
B.Roethlisberger 25
...
G.Smith 1
E.Manuel 1
D.Stanton 1
C.Kessler 1
```

There are a couple of cookbook examples for retrieving [sacks](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/wiki/Cookbook#calculate-number-of-sacks-for-a-team) and [sack yards](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/wiki/Cookbook#calculate-sack-yardage-for-a-team) for a team over the course of a game/games.

Those are aggregated on a play basis and are not reliant upon adding up any half sacks for individual players that might be assigned.

no games will appear in nflgame.games() until they are completed.  That query is looking for regular season games by default.  If you want to see the completed pre-season games (AFTER running the command you did to update the schedule), try this...

If you want to return a list of games not played, i've found the best way is to use _search_schedule.

import nflgame
s = nflgame._search_schedule(year=2017)

@garaud Welcome to the wonderful world of authentication!

Take a look at #34 

There is an example in there about how this sort of functionality might work once v 1.0 is rolled out (hopefully in a week or two).

In general, I am somewhat opposed to baking this logic into the token itself. Why? Well, the developer is given a lot of control over the refresh tokens since they are responsible for storing them somewhere, and then providing that stored copy back to the application when it needs it to verify that a user is passing the correct refresh token.

Therefore, this something that the application layer could handle itself.

```python
async def retrieve_refresh_token(request, *args, **kwargs):
    refresh_token_from_storage = do_something()
    if refresh_token_from_storage.is_too_old():
        return None
    return refresh_token_from_storage
initialize(app, retrieve_refresh_token=my_retrieve_refresh_token)
```

Perhaps I am wrong on this one. You are the second person to ask for something like this. Maybe in the future we will add an option to return a self-expiring refresh token.

For now, however, I leave you with two options:
1. Handle the expiration at the application layer using `retrieve_refresh_token`
2. Override the `get_refresh_token` by subclassing the `Authentication` class (on v 1.0), and run the check inside `retrieve_refresh_token`

Let me know if you have any other thoughts.

Got it working - but I've caught a couple of things

Your "cert=" call is actually enabling client certificate validation - which isn't used/supported by graylog-server until 1.2 (apparently - just read this yesterday). So it's not really needed in "traditional" mode where you just want the client to connect to the server. Only the "ca*" variables and "cert_reqs" are related to server-only validation

99.9% of graylog-server users would get a cert validation error using pygelf - as typically the server is self-signed. It's always best practice to default to "secure mode" - so your code is fine, but perhaps it would be worth documenting how to disable server cert validation for those who don't care (ie they want to use TLS as a *privacy* measure instead of a *security* measure)

I'm not a python coder, so I actually couldn't figure out how to properly make pygelf disable cert validation - I ended up hacking tls.py to change to cert_reqs=ssl.CERT_NONE to do that - but I assume I should have been able to do that from my script instead? (and strace then showed that the "cert=" file was never opened - verifying my above statement about client certs :-)

However, it still works! Awesome :-)

So I just noticed something, that for some reason hadn't stuck out to me: I'm using


```python
async with pool as connection:
    await connection.get(...)
```

and much of the interaction that I see in the documentation is as well as the aioredlock codebase now that I'm paying attention:

```python

with await pool as connection:
    await connection.get(...)
```

Much of the reason that I use a wrapper is to abstract away library specific interaction (and though common) - I prefer not to change my code base to conform to the `with await` structure.  I went ahead and refactored it like so:

```python
     import aioredis
     from aioredis import ConnectionsPool, Redis
     import config
    
    class RedisWrapper:
        __slots__ = ("_instance", )
        _pool = None
    
        def __init__(self):
            self._instance = None
    
        async def __aenter__(self) -> Redis:
            pool = await self.get_pool()
            self._instance = await pool.acquire()
            return Redis(self._instance)
    
        async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
            pool = await self.get_pool()
            pool.release(self._instance)
            self._instance = None
    
        async def get_pool(self) -> ConnectionsPool:
            if not self._pool:
                self._pool = await aioredis.create_pool(
                    (config.host, config.port),
                    minsize=5,
                    maxsize=10
                )
    
            return self._pool
```

So basically I'm retaining the interaction with 'create_pool' where my context manager is in charge of acquiring/releasing the the connection and I'm also retaining the ability to use the command mixins (as you mentioned -- which is exactly how I was originally interacting with aioredis before the upgrade) by feeding the connection as input into the Redis.

I believe this should work as expected as basically [`create_pool`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis/blob/ea948d0d7047db3315b4fbb9aa66e9ccc7e41522/aioredis/commands/__init__.py#L163) and [`create_redis_pool`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis/blob/ea948d0d7047db3315b4fbb9aa66e9ccc7e41522/aioredis/commands/__init__.py#L182) are having a baby: creating the pool (as done in create `create_redis_pool`) and feeding an acquired connection into `Redis` (as done in `create_pool`).  

Also I noticed something else : looking at the `create_redis_pool` as used in [`aioredlock` here](https://github.com/joanvila/aioredlock/blob/master/aioredlock/redis.py#L122) will get a Redis instance that [it performs`return await self._pool` on](https://github.com/joanvila/aioredlock/blob/master/aioredlock/redis.py#L128)) which in turn calls the (`__await__` function)[https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis/blob/ea948d0d7047db3315b4fbb9aa66e9ccc7e41522/aioredis/commands/__init__.py#L131-L140] from the `Redis` object. That's definitely safe for my currently envisioned uses, but that's mainly wanting to get your rpyc working so I can play with it. :) I don't know how other people use Plumbum. For example, I can imagine wanting to use it to ssh into my jailbroken iPhone, which has a reasonably complete BSD userland but doesn't have Python (although there is a mostly-working Python 2.5 package I could install).

But if it's acceptable, it seems like a great solution. And it also solves #180, and anything similar that comes up; Python provides a lot of functionality that's consistent across platforms that a POSIX userland doesn't guarantee. (It seems like you could even, with a bit more work, make `RemoteMachine` just work on Windows as long as they have an ssh server and a `python` on the `%PATH%`.)

One little thing: I think you want `dumps(dict(…), 2)` to force a protocol. That way a remote machine whose `python` is 3.x (as on at least Arch Linux) will still send back something you can parse on 2.3-2.7.

The `EOFError` is a bit general, since it means that the reply was not all that was expected. Plumbum works by the command you see above, it sends a request (like `echo $?`), followed by two echo commands with tags (The `END` things above), one to stdout, and one to stderr (Using `1>&2`). If your default remote shell does not support the redirection to stderr that you see here, you will get an EOFError. (Plumbum 1.6 will have a slightly better error message for this). Do your remote shells support redirection in exactly this form (i.e., are they bash?)

Take a look at [ECMA 5.1 specification](http://www.ecma-international.org/ecma-262/5.1/). Most of it is already implemented, but the execution mechanism is slightly different. I completely ignored their remarks about execution and took a different approach trying to be as compatible as possible. In my approach, I am generating a new Python code that should be equivalent to javascript. In interpreter approach, I will just write one Python script that is able to follow instructions in AST and execute them directly. So no python code will be generated. This gives more flexibility as I will, for example, be able to call a function with more than 255 arguments allowed by python, prevent recursion limit error and have behaviour that is exactly as defined in specification without using ugly python tricks. 

As a nice example `x++` translates to:

    `(var.put(u'x',Js(var.get(u'x').to_number())+Js(1))-Js(1))`

Interpreter will have postIncrement function that simply increments the value as described [here](http://www.ecma-international.org/ecma-262/5.1/#sec-11.3.1) .

Hi Arjan,

As in Excel one worksheet must be active and the active worksheet cannot be hidden.

From the [documentation](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-hide):

> A hidden worksheet can not be activated or selected so this method is mutually exclusive with the activate() and select() methods. In addition, since the first worksheet will default to being the active worksheet, you cannot hide the first worksheet without activating another sheet:

    worksheet2.activate()
    worksheet1.hide()

Here is an example:

```
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hide_sheet.xlsx')
worksheet1 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet2 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet3 = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet1.set_column('A:A', 30)
worksheet2.set_column('A:A', 30)
worksheet3.set_column('A:A', 30)

# Hide Sheet1. It won't be visible until it is unhidden in Excel.
worksheet1.hide()
worksheet2.activate()

worksheet1.write('A1', "Now it's my turn to find you!")
worksheet2.write('A1', 'Sheet1 is hidden')
worksheet3.write('A1', 'Sheet1 is hidden')

workbook.close()

```

I vote for `with xlsxwriter.workbook(filename) as wb: do_stuff()`. There's a sizable installed base of programs which expect the current API; breaking that breaks them. It's fairly simple to participate in the context-manager protocol (`with` statements). If you want to *also* support the idea of a "save-as" method, then I propose passing `None` to the constructor means you'll save-as later on.

Thanks for the report. That looks like a bug.

I'll look into it.

A few things that you can consider:

- The `write()` method is mainly a utility function and isn't intended for performance. If it isn't strictly required by your application I would recommend avoiding it and rolling your own `write_like()` method in you application or override it ([like this example](https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/blob/master/examples/inheritance1.py)).
- The underscore/undecorated methods are a good idea for performance. I had intended on making public versions of them at some point.

So let's give this a fresh start. I think we both agree by now, that it is not that simple. If you, @mathandy want to implement it in another way, that is totally fine. I just think it doesn't make sense for me to do it, if you already have very specific ideas about how you want it to be done. It will be more effort for you to tell me how you would like it to be implemented.

Although I am by no means a professional (I just code out of necessity for hobby and professional projects), I do care about coding style. Not about PEP8, but about thinking before implementing. So please let me explain my thoughts about the solution to this issue:

I was assuming that SVGs are typically composed the following way:

1. There is usually another group and/or a path in a group. So empty groups are rare in general.
2. Usually there is more than one path object in a group.

The file I had, had 200 paths in 86 groups nested up to four levels deep.

So for the sake of this argument, let's assume it has 200 paths in 62 groups in three layers. Then, in the first layer it would be 50 groups with 4 paths in each them, the second layer would be 10 groups of 5 groups of the first layer and the third layer would be 2 groups of 5 groups of the second layer.

I see two main ways of implementing the group transformations. Let me call them 'top-down approach' and 'bottom-up approach'. The top-down approach is what I preferred and implemented. It is recursively walking through the XML tree of the SVG and parsing the paths and groups along the way. I call this top-down because one goes from the root to the branch. In a hierarchy this is called 'top-down'. The bottom-up approach would be to walk through the XML tree once and identify all the path objects along the way. Then one loops over the parent nodes for each path to parse and apply the group transformations. This is parsing the groups starting from the leaves to the root. Hence 'bottom-up'.

As always, both approaches have their benefits: The top-down works best if the structure is deeply nested and if there are multiple paths in one group. However, empty groups are bad for performance. The bottom-up approach doesn't parse empty groups. Also it works well if there is one group per path. Therefore this approach is the better choice for shallow structures. One can also implement hybrid approaches, where the groups are parsed top-down and the paths independently of that. Then the transformation matrices are applied afterwards.

Apart from the thoughts about the best way to do it, there are also practical considerations, that play a role during the choice. The ones I see as most prominent are the following: In the current implementation the structure of the SVG file does not play a role so far. So it was perfectly valid, to extract the paths and work on them after they have been extracted from the XML tree. Since there were library functions available for the task, which can be assumed to be somewhat optimized, I agree that this was the best choice at that time. So to implement the top-down approach, one has to change existing code unnecessarily and the order in which the objects are returned by the function changes. The bottom-up had the clear disadvantage for me, that it was not the best approach for my example described above: in total, 600 group transformations would have had to be parsed and applied. In the top-down it is only 62 groups have to be parsed. Of course this is highly non-linear with the complexity of the file. In addition it allowed for all the other objects that are parsed into a path object, to be dealt with in the same walk through the tree. In principle there is only a small performance penalty for a hybrid approach. But there is a huge practical drawback: I was not able to come up with a good way to identify the individual path objects during the walk through the tree: paths usually have ID attributes, but you can not rely on this. The order in which minidom returns the objects in getElementsByTagName is not documented and therefore also unreliable (in the current version the elements are returned in order of appearance in the file). Of course one could say that it is unlikely that the order in which getElementsByTagName returns the nodes changes and rely on the current implementation. But still it would make the code more complex to read if two different approaches are mixed together.

Since for the svgpathtools the order in which the paths and converted objects are returned was not documented either and also was subject to change with minidom anyway, I decided this is undesirable but not a huge issue. The change of existing code was also undesirable to me, because it requires additional work, but in my opinion it was worth the hassle because it is the more elegant choice and it avoids walking the XML tree unnecessarily often and allows to directly cross-parse other nodes into paths in the same walk, which reduces the number of walks even compared to the current implementation without any groups. Also other complex features, like parsing the other group attributes and overwriting parts of the path attributes where needed, can be implemented in the same XML tree walk, if that is desired in the future.

Of course this is a question of style and therefore it comes down to personal opinions and preferences. Since you are maintaining the project it should be your choice. However it was not really clear to me from your comments, which approach you prefer. Is it the bottom-up or the hybrid one? I went ahead with the top-down approach, because it appeared to be the most logical one to me and my assumptions about SVG files. Also it complied best to my personal preferences, as I value simplicity, scalability and performance over keeping well-tested code. But as I said: it is fully up to you to decide which changes are unnecessary and which are not. I am just explaining here why I decided to implement it the way I did in my fork.

Sure, the rendezvous server is included in the distribution (run `wormhole-server --help` to get started). You can control which rendezvous server the client uses with a command-line argument (`--relay-url=XYZ`, where XYZ should probably be something like `ws://HOSTNAME:PORT/v1`). You can also select the "transit helper", which is the server that connects two TCP sockets together for bulk data transfer between hosts that can't make a direct connection, with `--transit-helper=tcp:HOST:PORT`. (the transit helper is also run by `wormhole-server`).

There's not yet a config file, nor is there an environment-variable override (although the latter would be pretty easy, since the `Click` argument parser we use supports it directly). What I do for offline testing is to have a little shell script named `wormhole-local`, which just runs the regular `wormhole` with `--relay-url=` and `--transit-helper=` args that point at my local testing servers.

```python
    def field(self, name, fieldType="C", size="50", decimal=0):
```

Numeric values in a .DBF file aren't stored as binary, they're stored as the character representation; so the `size` parameter is really a "character width" when serializing the value. 

So if by "integer attribute" you mean a signed 16-bit integer (for example), the "widest" value you'd need to be able to store is `−32767`, which has a width of 6 characters. So you'd define the field as:

```python
w.field('testattr', 'N', 6, 0)  # 16-bit int
```

A 32-bit long integer would need to store values as wide as `−2147483647`, or 11 characters:

```python
w.field('testattr', 'N', 11, 0)  # 32-bit long int
```

That said, there's nothing wrong with creating a numeric field too wide other than wasted space, so your default width of 50 characters should work fine for integer values.

@GlennV Thanks for report issue. I'm working on fix.

I think we should allow ``__slots__``. Although they are not mainstream, they are still used. I am however not sure about API that we should use. I think ``@add_slots`` still sounds like you patch an existing class. Maybe call it ``@with_slots``? Finally, maybe we still can use single decorator, but call the keyword ``with_slots`` to distinguish it from other keywords? My point is that people who will use ``with_slots`` are probably familiar with how slots work, so that they will not be surprised this option returns a new class.

The version of dataclass.py tagged with 'v0' implements the behavior of calling default.copy() if the default is list, set, or dict. That lets you write code like:

from dataclass import dataclass, field

    @dataclass
    class C:
        x: int
        y: list=[]

    c = C(3)
    print(c)          # C(x=3,y=[])
    c.y.append(10)

    d = C(42)
    print(d)          # C(x=42,y=[])
    d.y.append(20)

    print(c)          # C(x=3,y=[10])
    print(d)          # C(x=42,y=[20])

Which I think satisfies the "default should be safe", at least for by far the most common cases.

I think if we want to override this, we could add a "non-copy factory" sort of function, like:

    y: list = nocopy([])

We could also add a factory function marker to force a new instance for types we're not aware of. That is, something other than str, list, or dict:

    y: mytype = factory(mytype)

I'm thinking we could also add a way to specify a custom copy function, but I haven't thought it through yet.

So I guess the question is: if the common cases are safe and we have ways of dealing with non-common cases, does that make describing this too complicated, or too hard to reason about?

I thought about that before, that's why there's this presence of `import pyswarms as ps` that I "copied" from the `numpy` style. The only thing that's stopping me from doing this is that we'll have instances of this:

```python
import pyswarms as ps

ps.single.GlobalBestPSO # Good enough
ps.utils.functions.single_obj.sphere # Pretty long
ps.utils.decorators.decorators.cost # Kinda long too..
```

If it is possible to do `ps.sphere` or `ps.cost` but still respect the modules themselves, then maybe we can do something like that. We can load everything right away, but I'm just wary that we might have conflicting methods or something in the future.

That's the reason why I decided to have everything be loaded whenever needed. Atleast I know that users will always come for the high-level optimizers (and I'm sure the `cost` decorator too), but they won't really use all objective functions, and might clutter our namespace.

Right now, the design kinda follows that of Keras, where we have components separated into modules, and we load them whenever needed. The thing about `numpy` is that instead of components, you have operators which you will use regularly. It would then be a hassle if numpy does something similar to ours.

For now just explicitly declare `cost`, we can discuss this in the later `0.X` versions :+1:

I like the idea of having an easy way to run ffmpeg asynchronously and not wait for it to complete so that the data can be streamed via pipes.

Concerns:

**1.** I don't like the idea of having `run` return a different kind of thing when a certain magical input parameter is supplied though.

Would it make sense to have a `run_async` function that returns the pipes, while leaving the output of the `run` function as-is? That way if you call `run` you always know what kind of return value will come out.

Statically-typed languages generally disallow returning different kinds of things from the same function, and languages like python allow people to "cheat" and circumvent those checks, but it tends to be a bad idea.

Of course the code under the covers could be shared in some way so that a bunch of stuff isn't duplicated internally, but that's an implementation detail - I'm talking more so about what users of the ffmpeg-python API see.

**2.** I like that you added documentation for the new feature, but I don't think it should be in the top-level readme. The readme is already pretty long and I'd like to keep it concise so that people aren't overwhelmed when they're just trying to figure out how to use the library and have some idea of what to do with it.

How do you feel about moving it to the [examples readme](https://github.com/kkroening/ffmpeg-python/blob/master/examples/README.md)?  While I don't want to down-play the feature or make it hard to find, I do think that the people looking for it will be the kind of people who are willing to dive deeper into the documentation to figure out how to do it.  Then it doesn't burden someone who just wants to run a simple ffmpeg command without dealing with the complexities of pipes, but puts it in a place that's easy to find for someone who wants it.

You are probably updating quite often. When using many different values I would recommend updating less often.

For example:

```
import progressbar

bar = progressbar.ProgressBar()
for i in range(10000000):
    if i % 100 == 0:
        bar.update(i)
```

That only updates once every 100 values. The update method does quite a lot of operations so if you know that you'll do many updates this might help a bit.

Well if `pbar.min_value = 0` and `pbar.value = 0` when `pbar.finish()` is called and `pbar.value` is assumed to be in bytes for use of the FileTransferSpeed widget, then transfer speed should be:

`(pbar.value - pbar.min_value) / seconds_elapsed = 0` in bytes/second. Certainly not `8.3 B/s` unless my math is mistaken. I would also accept a value of `-.- B/s` as a valid option since no update has ever taken place which could be taken to imply that no data was transferred.

#1: order_by() works and it is better than using an arbitrary ordering.

#2: that's what I figured, and that's what Excel pivot does by default too (I think).

It's a valid argument for Sum, less so for e.g. Count. In practice, like in your shirt sales example, if a shirt of a particular style has not been sold, it will be represented with no row present in an inventory table. The total income for that style should ideally return 0eur.

3# I think that most SQL languages support the `OVER (PARTITION BY x ORDER BY y)` clause which is used by the Window function (see [here](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/window-functions-usage.html) for MySql). I have not tested it though, I use Postgres.

You cannot pass it as an aggregation because you need to call `distinct()` afterwards.
Basically instead of:
```
qs.annotate(**{column: aggregator(data)})
```

you need to call:
```
qs.annotate(**{column: Window(aggregator(data), order_by: F(row).asc())}).distinct()
```
It also requires some kind of support for passing ordering on the row field. Let me know if you want something more concrete.

This is and probably will always arguably be the most uncomfortable aspect of this method --- at least in the way it was implemented here. Just as a disclaimer, the goal of this package was to be simple and transparent, and to offer a fairly out of the box, plug and play, solution for BO. So I often made the conscious decision of leaving features and / or methods out for the sake of keeping it lean, and letting those who need more use packages such as spearmint.

Now to address your points:

1. One serious problem with limiting the parameter space BO has access to (which is the case when you don't allow it to probe 4.8) is that you may fall into an infinite loop --- I've been there. Say BO just probed 4.0, now it picks 4.2 as its next guess, if you force the value of 4.2 to 4.0 internally, BO will ask for 4.2 again on the next step, after all, its internal state did not change, all it knows is the value of 4.0 and 4.2 will still seem like a good choice.
Moreover this doesn't address the real problem that the underlying gaussian process, and hence the BO process, operate on a continuous space, while some parameters of the function being optimized are discrete.

2. Using round(x) is just as good of a choice, and it may make the process a bit more optimal, I haven't really tested one against the other to be honest. However I don't really expect big changes, the function being optimized is still C^0 (continuous but not differentiable everywhere), you're just changing where the step occurs (4.5, 5.5, ..., instead of 4.0, 5.0, ...).

3. Yes, updating `bayesOpt.res['all']['params']` will altear what the optimizer knows about your target function and it will accomplish what you are trying to do.

4. I agree that the entire discussion applies to `init_points` as well.

I understand that casting floats to ints (whether you round them or not) seems like a gross approximation, one that might even hurt the optimization process. Nonetheless I believe it is not that big of an issue provided it is addressed properly by setting the GP parameters adequately. Look at this function:
![download](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7664252/30749580-d5a3b634-9f81-11e7-8a7e-d051cbe8393a.png)
This is kinda of what you get when you set `int(x)` as is often done around here. It is definitely possible to approximate this function with a gaussian process, however in practice the choice of kernel and noise will be important, and may even determine if you're successful or not. 

Ultimately I rather pick a more flexible Gaussian Process (higher `alpha` and a kernel such as Mattern) and let BO do its thing, than try to restrict it

Well, we bypassed `flask-security` as we did not find any way to improve the speed. This was done by my colleague, so I can only provide some insights from what I see in the code.

He added a custom loader to `flask-security.login_manager`
```python
# add our custom callback for user token authorization
security.login_manager.request_loader(check_user_auth_token)
```

In the function `check_user_auth_token`, he implemented token deserialization via `itsdangerous.URLSafeTimedSerializer`, which, _and now I'm only guessing_, is faster than the implementation in `flask-security` but does the same job.

However, throughout our time with `flask-security`, we ran to so many issues (mostly related to its usage with REST API) that we decided to abandon it completely. Moreover, we are thinking about switching to Django and its Django REST Framework.

Not splitting on Windows is wrong, as per the [documentation](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/subprocess.html?highlight=subprocess.call#frequently-used-arguments):

> If passing a single string, either shell must be True (see below) or else the string must simply name the program to be executed without specifying any arguments.

For escaping the output/input filenames, [`pipes.quote`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pipes.html#pipes.quote) can be used.

You also lost part of the changes from 9bb70a470183bd7d40210b7687659edc2e99b052.

So I think a better patch would be:
```diff
 pyqt_distutils/build_ui.py | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git i/pyqt_distutils/build_ui.py w/pyqt_distutils/build_ui.py
index 8f51183..32287d6 100644
--- i/pyqt_distutils/build_ui.py
+++ w/pyqt_distutils/build_ui.py
@@ -4,6 +4,8 @@ Distutils extension for PyQt/PySide applications
 """
 import glob
 import os
+import pipes
+import shlex
 import subprocess
 import sys
 
@@ -84,7 +86,7 @@ class build_ui(Command):
                 filename = os.path.split(src)[1]
                 filename = os.path.splitext(filename)[0]
                 dst = os.path.join(dst, filename + ext)
-                cmd = cmd % (src, dst)
+                cmd = cmd % (pipes.quote(src), pipes.quote(dst))
                 try:
                     os.makedirs(os.path.split(dst)[0])
                 except OSError:
@@ -92,7 +94,7 @@ class build_ui(Command):
 
                 if self.is_outdated(src, dst, ui):
                     try:
-                        subprocess.check_call([t for t in cmd.split(' ') if t])
+                        subprocess.check_call([t for t in shlex.split(cmd) if t])
                     except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e:
                         if e.output:
                             write_message(cmd, 'yellow')
```

Hello miguel,

I think you were referring to the "ping_timeout" this is the value that as you have said makes the client terminates the server in which I have no issues with.  

The changes I did was more on the server perspective terminating a client which is the "ping_interval".

> If we make the assumption that the client isn't backed up (i.e. only the server is), then the client is sending the ping packets at the correct interval, and the responses to them are getting delayed.

Yes, you are right the client did send at the correct interval, however the server didn't even reach to the point to send a PONG response to the client because he already pass the "ping_interval". 

This scenario only applies if there's a backlog of messages in the packets. Let's say the following diagram where in d or data in the queue:

```ascii
d = data
ping = ping from client
t = time in seconds to n

           client ping at t(2)
            |      

       d1  d2   d3   ping
 t   --1----2----3----4------------------------------------------> n
```

In this example, the client sent the data correctly every 2 seconds. However, since there are so many backlog in the queue, server was able to process it at 4th second. This will make the server think the client has disconnected.
This issue has been fixed, so I'm closing. Please reopen if you still experience timeouts.

> Yes, currently we are testing the client's auto-reconnect feature and we had to set it on a low interval to simulate it.

Still not convinced this is right. Reconnections are an exceptional situation, that does not happen normally. The taxing on the system you are imposing by lowering from 25s to 2s is actually making it more likely that things will get backed up. I would actually go the other way, and allow pings to be sent less frequently. Maybe you don't realize that the client and the server do not rely on pings alone to detect disconnection. This is the method used when doing long-polling, but when the connection is over WebSocket, a disconnection is detected immediately, regardless how ping/ping packets.

Do ping packets get delayed when you use the default 25/60 interval and timeout?

1. Previously, any child `SQLAlchemySchemaNode`'s `class_` attribute was a SQLAlchemy Mapper, while the root `class_` was always (in my opinion, correctly) the mapped class itself. 2. These changes change the *default* behavior of `SQLAlchemySchemaNode`s to recurse into child models. Should we disable this behavior by default?

A correct usage would be:
```
Highlight    icon.PNG
#wait some time
Clear All Highlights
Click    icon.PNG
```

IMHO: There is a keyword missing, that represents the timed highlight in SikuliX:
`match.highlight(2)`
which would make the highlight auto-vanish after 2 seconds

I think you actually mean `STATIC_URL` variable. If so, then you should enable `django.template.context_processors.static` context processor to have this variable available in template context. However, this isn't related to `django-static-precompiler`, it's a feature of Django itself.

You can add `STATIC_PRECOMPILER_PREPEND_STATIC_URL=True` to settings. In that case the `{% compile %}` template tag (which is part of `django-static-precompiler`) will add `STATIC_URL` to the URLs of compiled files), so you don't have to add it yourself. Note that `STATIC_URL` is added *only* to the output of `{% compile %}` tag.

I believe there are two options:

b. in view's for auth, check request.data[alias] exists against a user  and if none:
1. pass the data to another view for creating a user

3. returning a success that the user has been created
4. prompt for login OR return a correct 6 dig token based on new setting LOGIN_ON_USER_REGISTRATION

`OAuth2` and many of its subclasses accept a `store_tokens` argument. See https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/master/boxsdk/auth/oauth2.py#L42  : 

```
        :param store_tokens:
            Optional callback for getting access to tokens for storing them.
        :type store_tokens:
            `callable`
```

You can pass in a callback which stores the refresh token into some sort of secure storage.

Then, when your app starts up, if there is a stored refresh token, it can pass it as the `refresh_token` argument to the constructor, and then immediately call `refresh()` instead of going through browser authentication.

@aidan-plenert-macdonald AFAIK this json is not intended to be easily readable by human and supposed to be used by mx.symbol.load function to recreate the graph.
That being said your net is pretty simple and that makes the json somewhat readable.
Go from the top and assign to each node an index starting from 0 as they appear from the top.
First node is input (index 0), second and third are weight and bias tensors for the first dense op (or in reality FullyConnected operation) which is fourth in the line (idx 3); this node has inputs node that tells you that it accepts three nodes as inputs 0, 1, 2 - input, weight, bias; Next in line (idx 4) is operation Activation (you created dense with relu activation) and it has input node 3 which is output from the FullyConnected op. This  way you can follow the logic to the bottom. But the json is not intended for this usage. Use vizualization module of mxnet to create a png of the symbol structure.

In the end, I worked around this problem by using a patched context. This context might be useful for other `asyncio` users. Note that my patched context is *not thread-safe*, unlike the original context. In my case, this is okay, since concurrency is achieved via the `asyncio` event-loop.

```python
class PatchedZmqAsyncioContext(zmq.asyncio.Context):
    """
    This patched zmq.asyncio.Context calls `destroy(linger=0)` during garbage collection instead of
    `term()`. This is a workaround for the weird hanging bug described in
    https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/issues/1003 and https://github.com/zeromq/libzmq/issues/2586

    .. note: This patched context is not thread-safe!
    """
    def __del__(self):
        """
        Instead of calling terminate as in the original zmq.asyncio.Context, we call destroy.
        This is not thread-safe!
        """
        if not self._shadow and not zmq.sugar.context._exiting:
            self.destroy(linger=0)

    def __exit__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """
        Instead of calling terminate as in the original zmq.asyncio.Context, we call destroy.
        This is not thread-safe!
        """
        self.destroy(linger=0)
```

FYI, just to make it clear, `tfcompile` does hardcode dimensions taken from the config file when generating the class. Problem is that the `aNFrames` (`n_steps`) parameter does depend on the input audio file used.

In `DeepSpeech.py`, we tackle that with a `-1` dimension, but that does not work for `tfcompile`:
```
batch_x = tf.reshape(batch_x, [-1, n_input + 2*n_input*n_context]) # (n_steps*batch_size, n_input + 2*n_input*n_context)
```

@revmischa I have been under the long standing misunderstanding that boto3 was a generic cloud services client? (e.g. - I didn't realize it was AWS only, and believed It was the standard python multi-cloud client library for AWS, Azure, GCE, etc.)

Looking through your script, it makes sense that you would be seeing that. What is happening is that for this part of the code:
```python
for instanceId in instanceList:
    instance = ec2.Instance(instanceId)
    count = 0
    maxTries = 3
    sleepTimeOut = 3
    while count < maxTries:
        if not instance.meta.data:
            result = int(maxTries - count)
            print("INFO: boto3 known bug trying again in " +
                  str(sleepTimeOut) + " seconds for " +
                  str(result) + " more time(s)")
            count += 1
            time.sleep(sleepTimeOut)
```
You are creating a brand new, unloaded Instance object on ``instance = ec2.Instance(instanceId)``. So if you access ``meta.data`` on the instance, the value will be ``None`` because there has been no API call made to load that data.

If you want to actually have the data available through ``meta.data`` I would recommend calling ``load()`` on the instance object or re-use the instances collected through the filtered collection. Typically the data get loaded if you access an attribute on the Instance such as ``tags`` that requires loading. The ``data`` attribute is a more lower-level concept if you are interested in the pure response that backs the resource and has no automatic loading concept on access.

I think you could consider creating a new gitdb issue if you have clear evidence on where the memory leak is created - usually this is hard to do, and more like chasing phantoms, especially in a GC language like python. Additionally GitDB/GitPython is in maintenance mode, and I am not investing any time in it unless it is possible to make fixes in 30m or less, which seems unlikely to be possible in this case.

A common fix for issues related with resources (like memory, filehandles) is to use the `multiprocessing` package to offload gitpython related work into its own process. That way, resources will definitely be freed. It's probably not worth doing that on a per-call basis, but one could keep these workers around for some time-period, and restart them in a certain interval.

Another solution could be to use the python bindings of libgit2 directly - it's somewhat lower-level, but depending on what you do it might still be feasible to make that change.

My personal preference would be to not keep the parent in the reference. The user should be aware it has a reference to another object, so it should be the user responsibility to keep the other object alive (eg, if you are extracting references from an array, then you are responsible to keep the array alive as long as you want to use the reference. If you want to get rid of the array, then deep-copy the content of the reference first).

I agree that documentation is scattered and in need of a good tutorial. The reason about mentions of remote webdriver for accepting desired capabilities, is historical. It started that way. I admit that Selenium2Library did not keep the same updating pace as the core library is being developed (and S2L is based in the Python port, which sometimes is a bit behind the Java core).

The solution you found is a low level workaround to get things done, and it is Robot Framework legit code. The Evaluate keyword evaluates Python code directly but needs to have the used modules referenced just like you would with the import statement. So, the code below creates a dictionary of current browser capabilities, then sets a new key and finally opens the browser (calls the create webdriver). This must be done in this order, capabilities can only be changed before calling webdriver.
```
${dc}   Evaluate    sys.modules['selenium.webdriver'].DesiredCapabilities.INTERNETEXPLORER  sys, selenium.webdriver
Set To Dictionary   ${dc}   ignoreProtectedModeSettings ${True}
Open Browser    www.google.com  ie  desired_capabilitie=${dc}
```

So we have this way to get things done, and because each browser have different "capabilities" or names, an all purpose solution is hard to create. One particular case is the PhantomJS browser that most of the times I must use the `Create Webdriver` keyword to pass some parameters.

@gsemet I get it, but everyone else has this problem too. If one of your dependencies updates in an incompatible way you'll either pin the dependency to a specific version or update your package to accommodate. The whole software industry has to deal with this. To make this easier we have [semver](https://semver.org/) so we can express things like: depend on package `foo >=3.x AND <4` to avoid getting incompatible changes pulled in. And we can always try to minimize the number of dependencies.

I understand that it might **sometimes** be necessary to vendor a patched version but vendoring **all** dependencies is certainly not the solution for reasons I already gave above. Just imagine a world where everyone else does the same: We'd soon have lots of code duplication everywhere and created us a nice little security nightmare.

I felt obligated to ask about a methodology/thought for identifying system level packages.  There are more than a few packages with actual system dependencies, not just python dependencies.  If you want massive adoption, adding this feature will be a huge net positive.  Also, I understand the level of difficulty this ask is.

+1 to extending python and building out a better setup.py and not another *file, because quite frankly, declarative system installs are pretty terrible as solutions once you have a moving install target.  I know everyone wants to make it simple, but it's just not.

-1 on developing yet another DSL with it's own set of rules when you have Python available.
-1 on using an esoteric and not well adopted format such as TOML.
-1 on JSON for the same obvious reasons

+1 on YAML.  The same arguments about indentation can be said about python.  YAML doesn't really change this aspect.  Presumably, the vast majority of full-time python developers have indentation down.  I'd argue some of the other problems related to YAML have more to do with poor or non-existent error handling within the yaml tooling and less to do with the format itself.  Decomposing or composing YAML has been done in both salt and ansible.  They're not shining examples, but given the other options, I really think YAML is second best after improving setup.py directly.  Just like code, though, YAML can be written well or not.

The annoying part with the existing state is that you can use `sub.items` as a setter but not as a getter:

```python
sub = stripe.Subscription.retrieve('sub_...')
sub.items = [{'plan': '10monthly'}]
sub.save()
# At this point, the subscription item is refreshed with the values returned by the API
# and the items attribute is reassigned to dict's items() function.
sub.items
# <built-in method items of Subscription object at 0x10f83fe30>
sub['items']
# <ListObject list at 0x11041aab8> JSON: {...} 
```

It's largely a matter of opinion, but I'd argue that preserving getter/setter consistency is more important than preserving the `items()` function (which IMO has limited utility outside of tests / debugging).

We could define an `items` property  to properly return the expected value, and it'd still be possible to access the `items()` method via `dict(sub).items()`.

A temporary workaround is to suppress NO_ZERO_DATE mode when starting mysql. Note that according to http://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/sql-mode.html#sql-mode-changes, this option will eventually go away and the behavior will become part of the "strict" mode.

This may be done either via `my.cnf` file (`my.ini` on Windows) or via the `--sql-mode` command-line option to mysql server. This is explained in http://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/sql-mode.html. You will probably first want to find out which sql modes are turned on by default, and then pass all sans `NO_ZERO_DATE`. `SELECT @@sql_mode` tells the current sql modes that are in effect

```bash
# exclude NO_ZERO_DATE
mysql_modes="ONLY_FULL_GROUP_BY,STRICT_TRANS_TABLES,NO_ZERO_IN_DATE,ERROR_FOR_DIVISION_BY_ZERO,NO_AUTO_CREATE_USER,NO_ENGINE_SUBSTITUTION"
mysql.server start --sql-mode=${mysql_modes}
```

I had this problem as well and there is already a way to customise both the *token payload* and the API Response payload.

 Basically, set the following settings in your **settings.py**

```python
JWT_AUTH = {
    'JWT_RESPONSE_PAYLOAD_HANDLER': 'custom_jwt.jwt_response_payload_handler',
    'JWT_PAYLOAD_HANDLER': 'custom_jwt.jwt_payload_handler',
}
```

You can define the payload in **custom_jwt.py**

```python
from datetime datetime datetime
from calendar import timegm
from rest_framework_jwt.settings import api_settings

def jwt_payload_handler(user):
    """ Custom payload handler
    Token encrypts the dictionary returned by this function, and can be decoded by jwt_
    """
    return {
        'user_id': user.pk,
        'email': user.email,
        'language': user.userprofile.prefered_language.url_param,
        'name': user.userprofile.name,
        'exp': datetime.utcnow() + api_settings.JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA,
        'orig_iat': timegm(
            datetime.utcnow().utctimetuple()
        )
    }

def jwt_response_payload_handler(token, user=None, request=None):
    """ Custom response payload handler.

    This function controlls the custom payload after login or token refresh. This data is returned through the web API.
    """
    return {
        'token': token,
        'user': {
             'email': user.email,
        }
    }
```

JWT are stateless so you shouldn't have any issues. On the auth server you would want your settings to look something like this:
```
JWT_AUTH = {
    'JWT_PUBLIC_KEY': <cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.rsa.RSAPublicKey object>,
    'JWT_PRIVATE_KEY':<cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.rsa.RSAPrivateKey object>,
    'JWT_ALGORITHM': 'RS512',
    'JWT_VERIFY': True,
}
```

other servers:
```
JWT_AUTH = {
    'JWT_PUBLIC_KEY': <cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.rsa.RSAPublicKey object>,
    'JWT_ALGORITHM': 'RS512',
    'JWT_VERIFY': True,
}
```

This will allow only the auth server to issue new JWT and the other servers to verify the JWT without exposing your private key. You will need to use the cryptography library to set the JWT_PUBLIC_KEY and JWT_PRIVATE_KEY. Documentation can be found [here](https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/primitives/asymmetric/rsa/#cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.rsa.RSAPrivateKey) and there is an example in the [tests](https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/master/tests/test_views.py#L339)

There's ample evidence to support the hypothesis that this project is abandoned. I've got a [fork](https://github.com/drmoose/pyjnius.git) which merges this and my two PRs to get something semi-usable, which is pip-installable via

```
pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/drmoose/pyjnius.git
```

I make no guarantees that my fork will be any better for your purposes than the official version, but it's working for my company for now. Unfortunately, I do not have the time to do proper maintenance and support on a project of this scale and complexity, so you're kinda on your own if it doesn't help.

I mention it here for @wavenator and anybody else who comes looking for a way to get pyjnius working on a modern OS, in the hopes that it may get you closer to something usable.

> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/kivy/buildozer/issues/135#issuecomment-121017392>.

There is an option missing in your buildozer.spec
First locate where you installed python-for-android and than add the recipes folder location to your buildozer.spec somewherebetween "[app]" and "[buildozer]"

For me python-for-android was located in `/home/kivy/Documents/firstproject/.buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/recipes ` and because `/home/kivy/Documents/firstproject` is my source.dir I added the following line to my buildozer.spec somewherebetween "[app]" and "[buildozer]":
`p4a.local_recipes = %(source.dir)s/.buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/recipes`
Thanks!

```python
import sys

# Dependencies are automatically detected, but it might need fine tuning.

setup(name = "Name",
        version = "0.1",
        description = "Description",
        packages = ["package"],
        options = {"build_exe": build_exe_options},
        executables = [Executable("package/main.py")])
```

To build for windows, I run `python setup.py bdist_msi`

My implementation would be something like this:

```python

try:
    from cx_Freeze import Executable
    class BdistAPK(Command):
        description = 'Create an APK with python-for-android'
	user_options = []

        def initialize_options(sel):
	    Initialize options

        def finalize_options(self):
            Finalize options

	def run(self):
            initScript = self.distribution.executables.initScript...
	    Call the ToolchainCL with info.

except ImportError:
    pass

```

Basically, if the user has cx_Freeze installed, the user will be able to use `python setup.py bdist_apk`. If not, nothing will be affected. If you want, I am willing to provide an executable class with similar parameters to the cx_Freeze executable class so that any user can run `python setup.py bdist_apk` even if cx_Freeze is not installed.

Perhaps a modified version of [cx_Freeze](http://cx-freeze.readthedocs.io/en/latest/overview.html) could be used? 

With cx_Freeze, you tell it which modules are needed for your app (already in the kivy requirements), then it scans all those modules and finds their dependencies (you can add ones it can't find automatically) and packs ONLY the needed python modules and their dependencies into a single `library.zip` file. Also it renames all .so files (only from required modules / packages) in the format `package.name.so` and places them in a single folder.

```
build/library.zip # all data, py/pyc files
build/libx.so
build/libpython.so
build/my.pkg.so
etc...
```

Once this is done:
1. The library.zip could be extracted and packaged in the apk (unzipped). Since the apk is a zip, if the apk were added to the python path, the python files could then be imported directly with the standard zipimporter (maybe would need a modified path).
2. All the necessary .so files could be copied to the lib folder so they are extracted upon install.

Pros:
1. Automatically remove all unused modules (thus reducing overall footprint, i've seen 3x-4x reductions on desktop apps)
2. Save space of unpacked python assets (current cost is  tar.gz size + unpacked size) as they would be loaded right from the apk (excluding .so files)
3. Remove startup time cost of unpacking assets on first run (since this would be done at install)

Cons:
1. Would need tested and implemented
2. Add a minimal startup loading time due to importing from zip (Importing the stdlib.zip from crystax only takes 89ms to load, so this may be negligible)
3. Loader would need to know to import so files based on dotted name instead of path.

I don't see a problem with input|about, Russian|longest and violence|anywhere|benefits  -- this is just a matter of zeroless representation.

sony|guys|fuck|pipe and cock|gang strings may deserve an explanation. Respectively, they consist of four and two separate words in the dictionary. Brotli's dictionary encoding is more compact than in c-strings: no zero bytes are necessary for marking the end of a string. The order in which the words are stored was chosen by an iterative algorithm that was optimizing for compression density. The earlier a word occurs, the less bits are needed to represent it. The words are not together in brotli's dictionary because of co-occurence in the training data, but the placement was driven by population counts, the first position of the word in a file, and by entropy modeling cost of the characters within the word.

The static dictionary data is not available in the brotli program as a human readable document, and does not affect brotli's ability to represent or not represent certain strings.

There is a conceptual problem here that has to do with the construction of the two formats. This is not a removal of zero length segments that would check if two consecutive points are the same and remove one of them (I think that's what you are referring to) . This function only checks the beginning of a section! Here is why this is a problem:

1) swc assumes that the points are actual points, so everything that is there and has an id is an independent point. 
2) h5 assumes (by construction) that points have to be duplicated in each section. In h5 the main objects are section not points.
