Hey Leonardo and thanks for the feedback. At the moment pip-accel indeed is not compatible (yet) with the 8.x releases of pip, I intend to rectify this in the coming days.

However usually you install pip-accel using pip and when you do so the `pip >= 7.0, < 7.2` requirement of pip-accel makes sure that (when necessary) pip is downgraded to the appropriate version. What likely happened here is that you installed pip-accel (and pip was indeed dowgraded) but then something else came along, upgraded pip to the latest 8.x release while being unaware of pip-accel and thereby broke the requirement.

This interaction is an an unfortunate side effect of the Python packaging ecosystem and tooling but I can't really do anything about it; pip and related tools will blindly upgrade any package you give it without double checking whether that breaks the requirements of already installed packages (as opposed to Linux distribution packaging systems based on tools like for example `dpkg` and `apt-get` which are way more strict in these things).

I could build in strict version checks within the pip-accel code base that scream at you to upgrade or downgrade pip but to be honest I'm not sure if that would be an actual improvement — I'd rather work on simply updating pip-accel to be compatible with pip 8.x :-).

Thanks for all of the feedback on this issue so far and note that I'm definitely hearing all of you loud and clear: pip-accel should be using the latest version of pip! I'm currently working on a feature branch... Some quick-ish notes about that:

1. I'm replacing the usage of `--no-install` with `--download` because `--no-install` is deprecated and will be going the way of the dodo. I'd rather use a supported feature instead...

2. Because of point one and because the pip download cache has been abandoned (they call it deprecated, but properly speaking the feature no longer exists IIUC) I'm forced to change the pip-accel source index into the functional equivalent of pip's download cache: In the old situation the pip-accel source index contained symbolic links pointing into the pip download cache, in the new situation the pip-accel source index contains the archives instead of symbolic links pointing to archives.

3. In my current (unpublished) feature branch all tests are passing using pip 6.0.6 *however* this is because I *temporarily* added the `--no-use-wheel` option (to gauge how much work it was to finish the upgrade). This explains why I'm not yet publishing my changes - I know that a big part of the newer versions of pip is support for (binary) wheels, so I assume that blindly ignoring that feature would disappoint a lot of users :-)

4. Adding proper support for binary wheels will undoubtedly be quite a bit of work, but I guess it will be worth it. I don't want pip-accel to stay behind in the dust of the Python packaging ecosystem, it should be able to take advantage of new initiatives like (binary) wheels.

To end on a high note, here's what the differences in my feature branch currently look like:

```
peter@macbook> git diff --stat master pip-upgrade
 Makefile              |    6 +--
 pip_accel/__init__.py |  142 +++++++------------------------------------------
 pip_accel/tests.py    |   13 +++--
 pip_accel/utils.py    |   20 +------
 requirements.txt      |    2 +-
 5 files changed, 28 insertions(+), 155 deletions(-)
```

Note the low number of insertions and the large number of deletions - I always like deleting code during an upgrade or refactor! One reason for this is that the pip developers have been listening to feedback regarding re-usability (amongst other reasons due to @hvdklauw's vocal support of pip-accel - thanks for that) which is much appreciated :-).

I plan to post an update here regarding my progress within a couple of days.

I just discovered the "Path to script executed on Guake start" preference, which was exactly what I needed, so I'm closing this issue.

For anyone reading this later, to make Guake start up with a pre-specified set of tabs ...

1. Create a script file (eg. /home/you/.guake-start.sh)
2. Add the following code to that script file:

    #!/bin/bash

    /usr/bin/guake &
    sleep 2 # let main guake process start and initialize D-Bus session
    guake -r "First Tab" -e "commandToRunToStartFirstTab"
    guake -n " " -r "Second Tab" -e "commandToRunToStartSecondTab"
    guake -n " " -r "Third Tab" -e "commandToRunToStartThirdTab"
    guake -s 0

If you want more tabs simply ad more `guake -n` lines for each desired tab.

3. In the Guake general preferences change "Path to script executed on Guake start" to point to the script you created in step 1.
4. Restart Guake: it should now open with the specified tabs

To start with `_body` is a private property and should not be used directly. At least one issue with the first code sample from @wollerman is that it should be using `raw_body` since that what is intended to be used for binary data. If you inspect the type of it under python 2 it should be a `str` and on python 3 it should be a `bytes`, as one would expect.

In order for API Gateway to treat something as binary it must be added to the binary content types list. So in this case you are transmitting binary through `multipart/form-data` so it must be added to the list. To get Chalice to do this for you, append it to the `binary_types` property like so:
```python
app = Chalice(app_name='s3-uploader')
app.api.binary_types.append('multipart/form-data')
```
On the next deployment that should be added to the types that API gateway considers binary. You can read more about that in the docs [here](http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#apigateway).

After that I got this working in python 3 by removing the usage of `_body` and the manually fetching of the content type header, and using the standard way of [getting headers](http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#Request.headers).

Python 3
```python
import cgi
import uuid
from io import BytesIO
import boto3

from botocore.exceptions import ClientError
from chalice import Chalice

app = Chalice(app_name='s3-uploader')
app.api.binary_types.append('multipart/form-data')
app.debug = True


@app.route('/upload', methods=['POST'], cors=True,
           content_types=['multipart/form-data'])
def upload():
    bucket_name = 'jcarlyl.test'
    request_body = app.current_request._body
    body = app.current_request.raw_body

    content_type_header = app.current_request.headers['content-type']
    content_type, property_dict = cgi.parse_header(content_type_header)

    property_dict['boundary'] = bytes(property_dict['boundary'], "utf-8")
    form_data = cgi.parse_multipart(BytesIO(body), property_dict)

    ext = 'png'
    extracted_image = form_data['file'][0]

    if len(form_data['file']) > 0:
        app.log.debug('got a file!')
        temp_name = uuid.uuid4()
        fullname = '{}.{}'.format(temp_name, ext)
        try:
            s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
            s3.Bucket(bucket_name).put_object(
                Key=fullname, Body=extracted_image
            )

        except ClientError as e:
            app.log.debug("ERROR: %s" % e)

        return {'status': 'success',
                'key': fullname}

    else:
        return {'status': 'error parsing file'}

```

I tested this by using curl.
```
curl -H "Accept: multipart/form-data" -X POST -v -F file=@hotdog.png $(chalice url)upload
```
Be sure to include the `Accept` header as API Gateway requires this header in order to properly parse the binary content types.

I then used the returned `key` property in the response to pull down and verify the image was the same as the one I uploaded.


This works just the same in python 2 except you can remove the line:
```python
property_dict['boundary'] = bytes(property_dict['boundary'], "utf-8")
```

The _coordinates_ and _transforms_ in `GWCS` are 0-based.
In `astropy.wcs` the transforms are 1-based while the coordinates may be 0- or 1-based and this is controled by the `origin` keyword.

In `astropy.wcs` when `origin=0` coordinates are shifted to match the `FITS` 1- based convention.
Because the transforms are also 0-based in `GWCS` they are equivalent to `origin=1` in `astropy.wcs`.

The `origin`  keyword in `astropy.wcs` is frequently cause of confusion and is often misunderstood. Since both transforms and coordinates are 0-based in `GWCS` we don't have to have equivalent keyword here.

>   Not sure on the specifics on this one, do we still, but if I would have to guess, we need to implement this in the `Bookmarks` object? 
> 
I don't think I've seen a use case yet that would drive the need for this. It sounds like a good thing to have and would be easy enough to implement, but I propose we wait until we have a live need for it.

While we're talking about use cases, have you thought about any higher-level use cases? Maybe captions would be a good place to start, so thinking through how a developer (or we) would use bookmarks to implement captions. So something like this added to the protocol section of the analysis document:

```python
>>> # ---add caption ---

>>> document.add_picture(...)
>>> document.end_bookmark(caption_bookmark)
```

...or whatever that would need to look like. I'm not sure where the caption text goes in but expect you've got an idea.

Based on these use cases, we'll be able to prioritize individual methods and properties to accomplish meaningful new capabilities rather than just nice-to-have ones :)

> - _Are bookmarks enforced unique?_
>    The word document allows for duplicate keys and it does not trigger a repair error if we there are.
>    However, we could implement a warning when aduplicate key is added?
> 

Okay, this is interesting. I wonder how it tells where the bookmark ends? I'd be inclined for us to adopt a "non-greedy" policy and identify the first-encountered `bookmarkEnd` having a matching ID as the end marker for a given `bookmarkStart`. This will be important when it comes to identifying the end marker of a bookmark.

In any case, we should make sure that we do not add any bookmarks with duplicate keys. An implementation of `.next_available_bookmark_id` should take care of that just fine.

Btw, did we already establish what, if anything, happens when the bookmark end appears before the bookmark start? This one's another for the experimental results (Word Behaviors) section of the analysis document

> - _Are overlapping bookmarks okay?_
>   yes, and in the current implementation, it works.
> 

Good to know. Let's make sure this is stated in the Word behaviors section of the analysis document.

> - _Complex type names._
>    The current implementation uses the names `CT_bookmarkStart` and `CT_bookmarkEnd` however, closer inspection of the XML schema excerpts shows that these probably need to be renamed to `CT_Bookmark` and `CT_MarkupRange` respectively.
> 

Yes. There are subtle differences in the case of the various XML-element-related names. The XML Schema names are always UpperCamelCase (with the prefix being all-caps, e.g. CT, ST, EG, etc.). These are always copied *exactly* when used as class names. Likewise lowerCamelCase for element tag names and attributes (and the variables that represent those elements and attributes). This convention provides useful visual cues for what kind of thing you're looking at in the code.

Try moving the call to `generate_copyfrom` after the call to `generate_methods`.

`copy_from` makes a copy of a stemmer, i.e. it copies all a stemmer’s fields. Therefore, the generated function must copy all the generated stemmer’s fields as well as the base fields. A more Rusty equivalent would be for the stemmer to `impl Clone`, which should be automatically derivable.

@jamalex ah okay so the PRs are actually bundled update suggestions :)

So what happened was this:

1. We got an initial upgrade PR with all upgrades combined. Then we didn't merge it, @pyup-bot got really annoyed and opened individual PRs while the combined PR was open still...

1. We got hit by individual PRs with updates, I merged some, closed some, and I've just rebased the rest.

1. @pyup-bot has opened a combined PR again for everything that hasn't been merged yet, but it has taken the new configuration and filters into account and left the individual PRs open. It looks like a daily routine. I think if we don't merge the combined PR, it will close it or update it tomorrow.

The individual PRs are still open, so I will process those as they complete successful builds.

So the current RulesGenerator needs to be improved to support the new services added recently (I need to tweak the header bar to be shared between both HTML docs) but you'll have limited ability to create your own rule set as follow:

./Scout2RulesGenerator.py --ruleset-name demo

This will open an HTML page in your browser, where you can browse by service and enable/disable/configure each rule.

The yellow / red configuration is the level drop down on the right hand side of each rule:
- yellow is "warning"
- red is "danger"

Once you've browsed through all your rules, click on the "Generate Ruleset" link in the top right corner, which will trigger a file download.

Then you can run Scout2 as you would have before, but specify the new rule set file:

./Scout2.py --ruleset ~/Downloads/demo.json 

If you already downloaded the configuration and don't want to re-fetch everything:

./Scout2.py --ruleset ~/Downloads/demo.json --local

> Can we include this in 1.6 milestone?

The code freeze for 1.6 is end-of-day Thursday. If this is mergeable by then, we will review it and include it in the release.

> Changes in the apps, like deployment of new versions or scaling, are not instantly reflated to the routers which results in routers sending traffic to dead containers.

This is by design, although I agree it's something we need to improve upon. Here's how it currently works:

* An application container is started with `deis scale`
* The `publisher` component, which is connected to the Docker daemon and listens to its event stream, detects that a container was started and [publishes](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/a7301968bba5b3b1d7f8cb6c278fc77fda14d294/publisher/server/publisher.go#L45-L69) it with a TTL of [20s](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/a7301968bba5b3b1d7f8cb6c278fc77fda14d294/publisher/main.go#L15-L16)
* The `router` component runs `confd` every 5 seconds, which templates `nginx.conf` with the app containers which have been published to etcd and then instructs nginx to gracefully reload

When an application container stops, publisher does not renew the etcd key's TTL on its next pass. Remember that these keys have a 20s expiry. Once the key expires from etcd, the router's confd (within 5s) re-templates the configuration and reloads nginx. 

So, there is indeed a delay from when an application container stops and when the router stops serving traffic for that application container. Note, though, that seeing a 503 in this case is expected, since there are no upstreams defined to service the request after the single container stops. The 502 is probably evidence of this "upstream existing but not responding" case.

Some thoughts:
* We may be able to tweak the publisher's TTL for keys as well as the router's frequency of confd updates - the router could update 1s or so which would increase load on etcd substantially, but perhaps this is acceptable to have better responsiveness for apps
* With a large number of app containers, perhaps the publisher loop through all containers takes too long? I could imagine a scenario where thousands of containers would take some time for publisher to renew all keys, in extreme cases even taking longer than 20s to get through them all

We investigated the performance of tsfresh under different parallelizations and data sets. One has to differentiate two cases:

1.) one has different kind of time series with low to medium number of samples
2.) one has only one to two kind of time series with medium to high number of samples

for 2.) the parallelization by id sample chunks seems to be faster (new). For 1.) the already implemented parallelization by kind seem to be faster (already implemented) and the additional parallelization by sample id is introducing so much overhead that it takes longer.

Here is a scenario. I want to send someone a signed message along with a pre-image that will perform some action in a contract IFF (1) the message is signed by me, and (2) the hashed pre-image matches the message that was signed. As a state-channel example, the recipient can only redeem her coins by providing the amount we've agreed upon (pre-image) and her permission slip to take them (signed message).

```python
    # Calculate two values for the pre-image
    # This could be any number of values. I wanted to show that by hashing them together
    # we get a nice fixed length byte-array that interacts well with the Solidity contract 
    addr = '0x' + '1'*40 
    value = 1
    # Hash these values to get fixed length of 32-bytes
    msg = Web3.soliditySha3(['address', 'uint256'], [addr, value])
    # Sign with a private key
    PRIVATE_KEY = b'\x11' * 32
    signed = Account.sign(msg, PRIVATE_KEY) #  Hashes "\x19Ethereum Signed Message:\n" + len(message) + msg
    # Create a dict containing the signed message and the pre-image
    # This is the important part I was hoping to save time with. These values all need to be
    # sent to the recipient. It would be nice to have a serialized version of the results from
    # `Account.sign`
    combined_data = merge(signed.serialized, {'addr': addr, 'value', value})
```

I like developing webapps with Flask or Django so here is a scenario where the server will sign the message:

```python
@app.route(methods=['GET'])
def my_endpoint():
    addr = '0x' + '1'*40 
    value = 1
    msg = Web3.soliditySha3(['address', 'uint256'], [addr, value])
    PRIVATE_KEY = b'\x11' * 32
    signed = Account.sign(msg, PRIVATE_KEY)
    combined_data = merge(signed.serialized, {'addr': addr, 'value', value})
    # Flask's `jsonify` and Django's `JsonResponse` accept a Python dictionary
    # Returns some kind of helpful server response object complete with correct json headers etc.
    return jsonify(combined_data)
```

What I am trying to achieve in this PR is (1) remove a bunch of boilerplate serialization code I have been including in my projects and reduce it to something like `signed.serialized` or `Web3.toSerial(signed)`, and (2) be able to merge those results with the primitive Python pre-image values. Keeping this step as a Python dictionary helps everything interact well with Python web-frameworks. Let me know if there is anything you're not clear on!

So, basically you make Python look in `/opt/lib/python2.7/site-packages/` for packages via the `PYTHONPATH` environment variable. Since the Miniconda installation has a completely different Python installation which has to look at `/d1/msaharia/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/`, it doesn't find its packages.
You'll just have to not set that variable (in your `.bashrc` or other files) and be good to go!
(If you can't figure out where that variable is defined in you setup, you can always run `unset PYTHONPATH` to get rid of it.)

@nicain:
> My suggestion, since conda is also a library, is to push the fix (the del of sys.path[0]) all the way down close to the entry points

That wouldn't help since `conda/__init__.py` and `conda/cli/__init__.py` will be loaded before `conda.cli.main:main` is executed. Hence, to guard as much imports as possible, the fix must be in `conda/__init__.py` and as early as possible.

@mingwandroid: `sys.path` will only be modified conditionally (esp. only if `sys.argv[1].startswith("shell.")`), so it's not as bad as it could be.

It think this is the right workaround if you'd want to do this inside the `conda` Python package. But another possibility would be to use custom entry points, i.e., replace
```python
import re
import sys

from conda.cli import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(main())
```
with something like
```python
import sys

# Before any more imports, leave cwd out of sys.path.
# see https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6549
if sys.path and sys.path[0] == '':
    # The standard first entry in sys.path is an empty string,
    # and os.path.abspath('') expands to os.getcwd().
    del sys.path[0]

import re

from conda.cli import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(main())
```
. This would add the workaround as early as possible, I believe.

Removing static to pcap_doc makes me think that the symbol is exported and is relevant out of the object, when it looks like an internal variable. Can you show me this compiler warning?

In regard to casting strings, the problem is the following: a string (like "pcapy.PcapError") is of const char* type. However, PyErr_NewException receives char* as its first parameter [1]. That's why the compiler is raising a warning. We have 2 options here: 1) PyErr_NewException wants to modify the string (and that's why const was not specified) or 2) const was simply ommitted (either because it was missed or because some compiler compiling a non C89+ C was not okay with this). I think option 2) is the most likely one. Compilers will put literal strings in read-only memory. So, casting to non-const makes no sense in run time: as soon as the string is modified, a segmentation fault will occur. My suggestion is to live with the warning. Sorry, I don't like it either, but the cast in the code looks even worse to me.

[1] - https://docs.python.org/2/c-api/exceptions.html

Currently VUnit does not use configurations as you know them in the VHDL language.

A "configuration" in VUnit is a way that a testbench can be configured to run, and typically all test cases are run for each configuration specified. A VUnit configuration does not generate VHDL, it only indicates things like -- how test files should be prepared (`pre_config`), any generics that should be set (`generics`), and anything that should be done after the testcase runs (`post_check`).

So in your example, by doing `tb.add_config`, you are adding a configuration for VUnit to test (which replaces the default). Internally VUnit will set the top level of simulation as the testbench entity (e.g. `my_module_tb`) and set its corresponding generic(s) that you define in the dict. All test cases that are part of the testbench architecture of `my_module_tb` will be simulated using the generic that was specified. If more configurations are added through `add_config`, then all test cases will be simulated for those configurations as well.

Here are the points which were relevant in my setup (please review thoroughly, I might have introduced inaccuracies):
- VUnit derives the compilation order from the list of files it is given, which thus should be complete. It then compiles each of these files separately, which means that each file needs to be self-contained, i.e. in the case of Verilog files, needs to have all used defines set. Defines that are set from the simulator need to be defined with the ``defines`` argument of ``add_source_file(s)`` so that VUnit knows about them.
- If your existing tests are not placed in the toplevel testbench module, you can set ``tb.scan_tests_from_file()``. In addition, you must propagate the ``runner_cfg`` generic/parameter through the hierarchy from the toplevel testbench to the entity/module containing the tests.
- Your testbench must not stop the simulation before ``test_runner_cleanup()`` (for VHDL) or ``TEST_SUITE_CLEANUP`` (for Verilog) because otherwise the correct simulator exit status will not be set.

```python
def my_session(session, config, **kwargs):
   """ Session is a candidate session already configured by msrest. 
   Config is the current client config.
   kwargs is on-the-fly arguments you can pass at call granularity.
   Return dict will sent as **kwargs to "session.request"
   """
   max_retries = config.retry_policy()
   session.mount("https://", SourceAdapter(max_retries=max_retries))
   session.mount("http://", SourceAdapter(max_retries=max_retries))
   return kwargs

client.config.session_configuration_callback = my_session
```

The problem occurred when I stored a std::string in a btAlignedObjectArray, then pointed the btHashString pointer to the c_str() pointer of the std::string. the btAlignedObjectArray copies over the elements when it resizes. Usually a string does a shallow copy (retaining the underlying buffer) if it can, but I guess sometimes it does a deep copy, de-allocating the old underlying buffer.

If the idea of letting btHashString owning the memory sounds OK to you, how about using strdup() instead?

cf the long discussion in ipython/ipython#4113.

The issue with this approach is that the numbering is reset with every render of a cell. If you have multiple cells with equations, then you wind up with redundant numbering. When every cell has a single equation, then each equation winds up with an equation number of 1 (not a very useful numbering).
![eqnumber](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5728311/28471365-c2a9f6ea-6df1-11e7-800b-f7c93771a7c8.png)
The conclusion reached in ipython/ipython#4113 was to decouple the two parts of this PR, and make equation numbering configurable (and probably off by default). Then you could make the equations have a global numbering. The downside of the global numbering (as you note), is if you try to retypeset an equation, it messes up the labeling and it won't render correctly at all. That is where the second part comes in: rather than try to clear the numbering before every typeset, just make it a user command accessible from the command palette. I probably agree with this approach.

> Since it seems something like field resolving order problem, is it possible to introduce ordering on dependency between fields into Schematics?

Manipulating data on import/export depending on what fields are present is already possible today. I'm just not really sure what it is you're trying to do here:

* Do you want the final model to have a field called `a` if the data had `"type": "a"`?

* Or do you want the final model to have a single field, let's say `submodel`, to hold the nested structure, regardless of what the type was?

**In the first case** there's nothing to stop you from defining one model that can accommodate several possibilities:

```python
class M(Model):
    type = StringType()
    a = ModelType(A)
    b = ModelType(B)
```

```python
>>> m = M({'type': 'a', 'a': {}})
>>> m.a
<A instance>
>>> m = M({'type': 'b', 'b': {}})
>>> m.b
<B instance>
```

The unused field will contain `None`. To prevent it from being exported, you can set `export_level=NOT_NONE` on the `ModelType` fields.

**In the second case** you would either use `PolyModelType` or the composition approach:

```python
class AMixin(Model):
    submodel = ModelType(A, serialized_name='a')

class AContainer(Container, AMixin):
    pass

class BMixin(Model):
    submodel = ModelType(B, serialized_name='b')

class BContainer(Container, BMixin):
    pass
```

This will cause `"a"` or `"b"` to be used both for import and export, but the model instance will only contain a field called `submodel`.

```python
>>> c = Container({'type': 'a', 'a': {}})
>>> c
<AContainer instance>
>>> c.submodel
<A instance>
>>> c.to_primitive()
{'type': 'a', 'a': {...}}  # `submodel` exported as `a`
```

Writing a `make_dict` is subtle because we might have to do two levels of unpacking to find Nodes: if we wanted to call our function like `make_dict([(key1, val1), (key2, val2)])`, then we need to handle the argument being a list of Nodes or a list of pairs of Nodes (or a mixture of the two). That is, each `(key, val)` pair may be a SequenceNode, or `key` and `val` may separately be nodes paired up in a Python tuple or list.

I think one solution is to have a non-primitive `make_dict` that first unpacks down to the level of individual `key`s and `val`s, so that we know which case we're in, and then have a primitive `_make_dict` that yields a DictNode. Here's one version, though it can probably be improved:

```python
def make_dict(pairs):
    keys, vals = zip(*pairs)
    return _make_dict(make_list(*keys), make_list(*vals))
@primitive
def _make_dict(keys, vals):
    return dict(zip(keys, vals))
_make_dict.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, keys, vals: [g[key] for key in keys],
                  argnum=1)
```

A DictNode won't like mutating assignment, so you'd want to replace lines like this:

```python
for k in x.keys():
    x[k] = x[k] - 0.1 * grad_dict[k]
```

with things like

```python
x = make_dict([(k, x[k] - 0.1 * grad_dict[k]) for k in x])  # untested!
```

Yeah, I think that would be cleaner/more flexible on the user side, and I would imagine the performance overhead is probably negligable, though not sure?

I guess my approach allows/forces you to be explicit about the order in which things will be saved/deleted, I don't know whether this is good or bad. Just looking at your example code, I'm guessing the left hand `bar` in `bar(x, x/3.) + bar(x, x**2)` will be executed first, but I would have to check on the python docs to make sure. The order might be very important if you wanted to go hard-core on the checkpointing and use all your available memory, but maybe it isn't necessary to force people to be so explicit.

One thing that's a bit annoying is that `make_jvp` doesn't currently work on functions like [this](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/pull/182/files#diff-d9c4c201a2dd339280c7995d37479ce6R147) which return a tuple (is this related to array insertion not being supported?). I thought being able to return a tuple would be handy in this function-centric way of controlling checkpointing. For example, if you are running an RNN whose hidden state is comprised of two arrays of different sizes, both of which needed to be passed forward to do the next bit of computation, I think you need to have a function which returns both of the hidden states. This is why I adapted and used `vector_jacobian_product`.

It might help to have one or two more concrete examples of how we might apply checkpointing. I had in my head something repetitive like an hmm or rnn when I wrote the implementation in the pr.

I'm not familiar with using the kmip4j client. However the error you're seeing indicates that the likely problem is an SSL/TLS mismatch. The PyKMIP server will only establish TLS 1.0/1.2 connections, depending upon the version of Python being used. If the kmip4j client can only establish SSL connections it will not be able to connect to the server.

Right now there is no way to disable TLS for the PyKMIP server. If the kmip4j client cannot use, or be upgraded to use, a more secure version of TLS it will not be able to connect to the server. However, this would also be true if you were using this client to connect to a certified KMIP appliance. To be KMIP compliant, an appliance *must* only support TLS 1.0/1.2. Anything else is insufficient.

This appears to be an environment configuration issue.  If you have installed and can locate the shared library file (libcliv2.dylib) and are getting that `Library not loaded` message then it usually ends up indicating that something about the library search path OR that something is wrong with the architecture (like 32-bit vs. 64-bit, but I would doubt this is that since macOS will be on mostly the same arch).

I know very little about macOS, but maybe try setting the `DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH` to something like:

```
export DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
```

And also the NLSPATH doesn't need to append NLSPATH again at the end so you can strip the `:$NLSPATH`.

I agree that making `use_venv` the default is the way forward. There doesn't seem to be a way to control pip the way we need, and we don't want to rewrite pip.

The issue here is that a person updates their reactive charm, and it breaks during deployment. They are being victimized by subordinate charms that are unchanged.

If we can get a build time warning, that would be good and mostly address the immediate issue.

I do wonder if we should just make it the default now, since we are already starting to see incompatible API changes. We have concerns about problems people might have, but it would solve we know people are having.

The `charms.reactive` cli command could certainly automatically use the correct interpreter using a shim or wrapper.

Hello,

The support regarding formatting modification isn't very well integrated into RedBaron yet but it's accessible. The way to go to find this kind of information is to use `.help()`. But, formatting informations aren't displayed by default in `.help()`, for that, you'll need to use this paramter: http://redbaron.readthedocs.org/en/latest/basics.html#with-formatting

Which gives this in your case:

```python
In [2]: RedBaron("a = 1").help(with_formatting=True)
0 -----------------------------------------------------
AssignmentNode()
  # identifiers: assign, assignment, assignment_, assignmentnode
  operator=''
  target ->
    NameNode()
      # identifiers: name, name_, namenode
      value='a'
  value ->
    IntNode()
      # identifiers: int, int_, intnode
      value='1'
  first_formatting ->
    * SpaceNode()
        # identifiers: space, space_, spacenode
        value=' '
  second_formatting ->
    * SpaceNode()
        # identifiers: space, space_, spacenode
        value=' '
```

Therefor:

```python
In [7]: RedBaron("a =\\\n    2")[0].second_formatting
Out[7]: 0   '\\\n    '
```

Hope that helps,

Hello @patrickleboutillier 

Sorry for taking so long. It took me a while to find, what I think is, the cause. Below follows the message from PR #174, for convenience.

When listening on all addresses (e.g. `--listen 0.0.0.0`) and using `--dns`,
DNS will stop working locally.

This happens because we install a `REDIRECT` rule that sends all outgoing
traffic to port 53 (`--dport`) to the sshuttle udp port on the loopback address.

Later when the response comes back from the server we use that same socket to
send the reply to the original application.

If the socket is bound to loopback, the NAT in iptables that matched earlier
will correctly translate back the source. However if the socket is bound
to all addresses, the response which has as destination the IP from where the
packet would have left the machine, won't be delivered to the application via
loopback but instead via the interface with that address.

Given the above, the iptables rule won't match and the source won't be
translated. The packet is delivered to the application with an unexpected
source and gets dropped.

Unfortunately the change I submitted will not solve this problem, but rather detect when
the user requests `--dns` and `--listen` on all interfaces, emit a warning and
make the DNS proxy bind to localhost. In this case DNS will continue to work
locally, however remote machines trying to redirect DNS traffic to sshuttle
won't be able to do so anymore, unless `--listen` is provided with a specific IP
address.

I would be happy to implement any other ideas, I just can't think of anything
that would properly resolve this right now.

@patrickleboutillier @brianmay Any other ideas on how to solve this?

Thanks!

In general, I support the approach of having users download a single script instead of cloning our entire repo.

With that said, I think anyway we go about this is going to increase complexity and make it harder for our users to understand what the script is doing. The two approaches I've seen proposed so far is this and the `letsencrypt-auto` super script that includes all of the OS bootstrap scripts as well.

If we did the latter, I think the plan was we would ideally write a script that pieced together the OS bootstrap scripts and `bootstrap/venv.sh`. We could have each script be a function in the super script and just piece together the functions accordingly.

Between the two approaches, I think the large bash script is easier to understand as the user doesn't have to track down all of the scripts that were run on their system. With that said, this approach proposed here is easier for us to implement and gives us more flexibility of updating the script in the future. Neither approach is completely self updating though as the initial script the user runs (correctly) doesn't try to update itself.

Despite the extra effort involved, I personally am leaning towards a cleanly written and documented `letsencrypt-auto` script with the OS bootstrap code included. With that said, I think either of these approaches is better than having users clone our entire repo.

I think the only mildly surprising thing in this list is `mmddyyyy`, which I think is a very uncommon way of expressing a datetime. I vaguely recall discussing this on another issue at some point, but I don't recall the resolution. It seems unlikely that fixing it will cause any problems in the common case, since most datetimes will be within +/- 100 years of today, and that will unambiguously select the last 4 digits as `yyyy`, and the first 4 digits can be disambiguated using `yearfirst` vs `dayfirst`.

As for times with > 24 hours, that's to be expected, since `datetime.time` is bounded between `00:00:00.0000` and `23:59:59.999999`, and real times (when considering leap seconds) have an upper bound of `23:59:60.999999`.

I'm not sure I agree that comparison should be implemented such that it returns `True` or `False` in that way, since I don't think it really makes sense to compare to `relativedelta` objects like that. Consider, what should be the answers to the following comparisons?

```python
relativedelta(months=1) < relativedelta(days=30)
relativedelta(months=5) < relativedelta(day=9, hour=2)
relativedelta(weekday=MO) < relativedelta(weekday=FR)
```

Basically, because `relativedelta` doesn't really map to any fixed time period, it doesn't make much sense to directly compare them.

@binnisb What version of `dateutil` and `python` are you using, specifically? I would expect `relativedelta` to raise a `TypeError`, not return `False`, and that's what I get with Python 3.6.0, dateutil 2.6.0:

```python
>>> from dateutil import __version__
>>> __version__
'2.6.0'
>>> from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
>>> relativedelta(months=3) < relativedelta(months=4)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-2-cf9dd5f49f4d> in <module>()
----> 1 relativedelta(months=3) < relativedelta(months=4)

TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'relativedelta' and 'relativedelta'
```

This is a duplicate of issue #58. The resolution we decided upon then was to add `OverflowError` to the documentation as a possible failure mode, as this is the normal behavior of the datetime module. It's still not clear to me why datetime raises `OverflowError` when `OverflowError` would only be raised in situations where the number would raise `ValueError` *anyway*, so I'm open to catching this error and re-raising it as a `ValueError`, but on the other hand, I'm not totally comfortable disguising the source and type of errors raised in this context.

Also, the optional `[a|b]` isn't strictly the same as what `(a|b|)` would be: if optionals used a simple regexp like that, the user would require hitting space twice when they don't want to include the optional.

```python
Python 3.5.2 (default, Sep 14 2016, 11:28:32) 
[GCC 6.2.1 20160901 (Red Hat 6.2.1-1)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import re
>>> trigger = re.compile(r'^what is your (home|cell|) phone number$')
>>> trigger.match("what is your home phone number")
<_sre.SRE_Match object; span=(0, 30), match='what is your home phone number'>
>>> trigger.match("what is your cell phone number")
<_sre.SRE_Match object; span=(0, 30), match='what is your cell phone number'>
>>> trigger.match("what is your phone number")
>>> trigger.match("what is your  phone number")
<_sre.SRE_Match object; span=(0, 26), match='what is your  phone number'>
>>>
```

Slightly more accurately, `[a|b]` would be equivalent to `(?:a|b|(?:\s|\b))`, with the "empty" value being actually a whitespace or word boundary so that the input message doesn't need two spaces to match the missing optional.

Some additional remarks about bbox support and ForecastModel:

I tried implementing bounding box support myself but gave up and just wrote my own script using siphon to get the data I needed. I borrowed a lot from ForecastModel -- constructing the query, converting the NCSS dataset to a DataFrame, etc -- but it was clear to me that too many changes were needed with ForecastModel to make it all work:
* `ForecastModel` assumed one `Location` for the whole forecast. 
* `process_data` uses that one location for any solar position stuff
* THREDDS rejects requests that provide a vertical level and variables that don't exist at that level. THis isn't a problem with single point queries -- you can request e.g. surface temperature and then wind speed at 100000 Pa. But with a bbox, TRHEDDS will complain that the vertical level doesn't apply to all variables requested. So, you either have to avoid that situation by only requesting variables with the same level range (or no range at all), or perform multiple THREDDS requests, one per level needed (e.g. 1. wind speed at 100000 Pa, 2. surface temperature).

Related note: I think ForecastModel should be broken up into a few collaborators. E.g., converting raw data to processed data could be in another module entirely; why does it need to know that the data came from a NetCDFSubset? Also, instead of subclassing, it would be great to be able to instantiate ForecastModel with (a) a siphon NCSSDataset object and (b) a dict of variable name mappings. This would enable users to use other THREDDS servers, e.g. the [GFS historical archive](https://rda.ucar.edu/thredds/catalog/files/g/ds084.1/catalog.html?dataset=files/g/ds084.1) at UCAR . Not sure if there's an issue open about this but it could be pretty useful for some people.

I've just looked through the code for our Guzzle-based PHP client that uses the OAuth2 password flow. We have a sequence of steps that run roughly like this:

1. Look at cache for a valid token
2. if no cached token, then POST an application/x-www-form-urlencoded form to the authorization endpoint to retrieve a new the token
3. cache the token
4. send the request with the obtained token as a Bearer HTTP header

Obviously this is just one kind of flow and the plugin system would need to be flexible enough to handle a range of different scenarios. I guess we shouldn't try to be too prescriptive and instead give the authentication plugin pretty much free rein to do whatever it needs to set up the request properly for authentication.

We may also need some kind of hook that can detect a 401 error and then try to get a new token and re-send the request. This commonly happens when a locally cached token is believe to be valid but the endpoint has decided that the token has expired. In these scenarios it's nice if the client is able to handle all of this internally and re-try with a new token without returning an authorisation error to the calling code.

In terms of workarounds or interim solutions, I've noticed that I can pass in credentials to the client via the transports list. This is just a simple test, but this authenticates me okay to my local dev server.

```
transports = [
    HTTPTransport(credentials={"localhost": "Bearer xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"})
]
client = Client(decoders=decoders, transports=transports)
```

Is there any chance you could explain a little more about the credentials variable/attribute and the purpose behind the host check? I'm just wondering if I can find a way to piggy back on this somehow. Perhaps the token generation and caching could be done outside of CoreAPI for now and the client is then instantiated with a transport that has a bearer token ready to go.

It's not ideal but it might be okay as a workaround until https://github.com/core-api/python-client/issues/115 is complete.

Z3 has tactics that are constructed from more basic elements (both sat solvers being such) and they will be selected based on what appears in your problems, usually by simple features, as the newer SAT solver doesn't support some features that the old one does support, but the old SMT solver does support everything (including SAT). For example, for QF_BV problems it runs the [qfbv tactic](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/smtlogics/qfbv_tactic.cpp#L102), which will select the `sat` tactic (often fast) or the `smt` tactic (often slow, but supports uninterpreted functions). If there is anything in the problem that's not in QF_BV, then the `mk_is_qfbv_probe()` will detect that and run the fully-featured SMT solver instead of the pure SAT solver.

If you want full control, you can build such tactics by yourself, either directly in an SMT2 file, or via the API, see the [strategies tutorial](http://rise4fun.com/Z3/tutorial/strategies). There is no option for switching between those two solvers, because the choice between them is usually made based on feature support and not based on performance considerations.

Ok, it seems my implementation precluded an inplace optimization for the running mean/var updates. With the following variant, performance is equivalent to the current `BatchNormDNNLayer`. So there's nothing to gain from letting cuDNN perform the updates of the running mean and variance, so we don't need to change the interface.

Note that we should still use `T.nnet.bn`, but in the same way it's currently done in `BatchNormDNNLayer`.

<details><summary>Improved `BatchNormLayer` implementation compared to the previous post.</summary>

```python
class BatchNormLayer(lasagne.layers.BatchNormLayer):
    """
    Implementation of BatchNormLayer for recent Theano versions
    """
    def __init__(self, incoming, axes='auto', epsilon=1e-4, alpha=0.1,
                 beta=init.Constant(0), gamma=init.Constant(1),
                 mean=init.Constant(0), var=init.Constant(1), **kwargs):
        super(BatchNormLayer, self).__init__(
                incoming, axes, epsilon, alpha, beta, gamma, mean, var,
                **kwargs)
        self.var = self.inv_std
        del self.inv_std

    def get_output_for(self, input, deterministic=False,
                       batch_norm_use_averages=None,
                       batch_norm_update_averages=None, **kwargs):
        # Decide whether to use the stored averages or mini-batch statistics
        if batch_norm_use_averages is None:
            batch_norm_use_averages = deterministic
        use_averages = batch_norm_use_averages

        # Decide whether to update the stored averages
        if batch_norm_update_averages is None:
            batch_norm_update_averages = not deterministic
        update_averages = batch_norm_update_averages

        # Theano requires beta/gamma tensors; create dummies if needed
        shape = tuple(s for (d, s) in enumerate(input.shape)
                      if d not in self.axes)
        gamma = self.gamma or theano.tensor.ones(shape)
        beta = self.beta or theano.tensor.zeros(shape)

        # obtain self-normalized outputs and statistics, if needed
        if update_averages:
            # Trick: To update the stored statistics, we create memory-aliased
            # clones of the stored statistics, and give them a default update
            running_mean = theano.clone(self.mean, share_inputs=False)
            running_var = theano.clone(self.var, share_inputs=False)
            (normalized, input_mean, input_inv_std, new_running_mean,
             new_running_var) = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_train(
                     input, gamma, beta, self.axes, self.epsilon,
                     self.alpha, running_mean, running_var)
            running_mean.default_update = new_running_mean
            running_var.default_update = new_running_var
        elif not use_averages:
            (normalized, input_mean,
             input_inv_std) = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_train(
                     input, gamma, beta, self.axes, self.epsilon)

        # normalize with stored averages, if needed
        if use_averages:
            normalized = T.nnet.bn.batch_normalization_test(
                    input, gamma, beta, self.mean, self.var, self.axes)

        return normalized
```
</details>

tl;dr -- the way ncclient currently receives messages can't let you do what you want without substantial changes.

When receiving messages, the IPC reading code just pulls bytes off of the SSH channel, and looks for either the 1.0 or 1.1 message delimiter, depending on which was selected after the hello. The 1.0 and 1.1 parsing code each do this a little but differently. For 1.0, it just appends to a StringIO buffer until it sees the delimiter, and for 1.1 it builds a list of received chunks. In both cases, the final message is coalesced just before dispatching the message back to the logical session that has knowledge of the outstanding RPCs.

This means that the receiving code has no idea which RPC it is currently reading data for, so it has no way to link the bytes while reading them back to your original RPC. Without major changes, the best that could be notified would be some kind of "I just read N byes from this SSH session", probably to a new "activity listener" or something. And this wouldn't give any _direct_ insight into which RPC the bytes were relevant to or how many more bytes are needed to complete the message. In effect, you'd know that something was happening, but not what.

Also, if one of your goals of moving to async operations is to look at leveraging pipelining, potentially with multiple outstanding operations, then the link from your original request to the bytes currently being received becomes even more tenuous without the inclusion of more bookkeeping infrastructure that would also depend on the underlying server honouring the ordering constraints detailed in [Section 4.5 of RFC 6241](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6241#section-4.5).

One thing I did think about was freezing the buffer currently being appended to and provide a copy back to your code. This freeze & copy would be necessary because the receiving code runs on a separate thread, and so we cannot give direct read access. And, again, unless you parsed this buffer yourself, you'd not know if it was anything to do with your current RPC. Also, the performance hit of doing this for large messages would be substantial.

Given this, I don't think what you are looking for is achievable without fairly major surgery on the code, and rethinking many aspects of how messages are processed. An optional "activity listener", with the limitations I noted above, is probably the only simply added approach.

Cheers,

Einar

So, here is the plan for the api for holding content to be displayed now. It is important that this is well designed in advance, because a lot of mpsyt's code will then be ported to use this api.

There will be a `Content` class, which may be abstract or could have some simple default implementation. Child classes should have a `get_content()` method, which should be a generator (or other function returning an iterator) returning each item to be displayed. Results of this will be cached, and the next item retrieved from the iterator as needed.

Originally the idea was for these items to be text lines ready to be displayed, but that won't work so well for code that adjusts to window width, etc. So they could be in any format, but another method is needed to process them, the `format_item` method (if this sounds terrible, please do suggest a better design).

That function takes the item retrieved from the iterator and the width of the screen (other parameters as well?), and should return a string to be displayed on the screen.

There will also be a `TabularContent` class, a subclass of `Content`, for displaying content in tables, as is often used in mpsyt.

I am still not confident with this design. For one thing, tables could be displayed better using other methods in e.g. an urwid-based curses interface, which would not be possible using this purely text-based method.

Also, there are another two optional methods of `Content` subclasses, the `get_length()` method, to return the number of items if that is known in advance, and the `get_message()` method, for a message to display at the bottom of the screen if needed.

Any comments or suggestions?

When I provision a URI like this (with the issuer string **exactly** as I wish to see it):

`uri = totp.provisioning_uri("tjgerot@example.com", "Hello World")`

It generates the following URI (notice it replaces the space with a plus sign at the end):

`otpauth://totp/Hello%20World:tjgerot%40example.com?secret=KQKHMS5UA3ATFSHO&issuer=Hello+World`

![wrong](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9205510/26755598/ed460950-4855-11e7-86f6-3160f540754f.png)

If I make this into a barcode, this is how the top 4 major apps will render the issuer:

Google Authenticator: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:
Authy: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:
LastPass Authenticator: `Hello+World` :x:
OTP Auth: `Hello+World` :x:

As you can see, only **some** applications are willing to accept plus-encoded labels. I propose we switch to percent-encoded labels which **all** applications accept and decode.

If this: `uri = totp.provisioning_uri("tjgerot@example.com", "Hello World")` would return this (`%20` instead of `+` at the end):

`otpauth://totp/Hello%20World:tjgerot%40example.com?secret=KQKHMS5UA3ATFSHO&issuer=Hello%20World`

![right](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9205510/26755607/15dcc340-4856-11e7-952b-6aeaaab26fa5.png)

all of the major applications accept this and render the issue labels as such:

Google Authenticator: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:
Authy: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:
LastPass Authenticator: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:
OTP Auth: `Hello World` :heavy_check_mark:

What it comes down to is the function could either provide an encoding that works for **some** apps, or one that works for **all** apps.

Hi @sogartar ,

The SDK provides 2 main APIs for users:
1. `rs2:context` + `rs2::device` + `rs2::sensor`
2. `rs2::pipeline`

The second API is a facade (simpler API) over the first one.
It is not recommended to mix to two together but for advanced stuff using the pipeline is just not enough and you'll have to use the first set of APIs.

Regarding your first question about multiple pipes:
First, let's have some common terminology: 
* A device is a collection of sensors. (e.g: D400)
* A sensor is a collection of streams (e.g: Depth sensor which is comprised of IR+DEPTH)
* A stream represents the flow of a single data type (e.g: IR / Depth / Color stream)
Read more on this in the documentation of the [`rs-sensor-control` example](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/examples/sensor-control).

The pipeline is (at the moment) a wrapper of a device for simplified usage which removes the boilerplate code required to start streaming. A single pipeline uses a single device (once it is started - after you stop it the pipeline can use a different device if configured differently).

Technically I don't think we prevent creating multiple pipeline which use different streams of the same device, the only restriction is that "start" (of streams) is at the sensor level - meaning that all streams of the same sensor must be started together (via the sensor's start function) and stopping the sensor will stop all streams. So if you have 2 pipelines , 1 D400 camera and you plan to start IR from 1 pipeline, and DEPTH form another pipeline - you will fail (because the D400 has a single sensor that has DEPTH and IR streams).

From your first question it looks like you want to have more control over each sensor (e.g get IR+DEPTH frame on 1 thread and COLOR frames on another thread), if the is the case don't use the pipeline and follow the `rs-sensor-control` example.

Regarding your second comment:
The `rs2::config` serves as a configuration parameter for the `rs2::pipeline`. You can think of it as a collection of your requests / required restriction that you want to have the pipeline to make.
The usage should be:
* create a `rs2::pipeline`
* create a `rs2::config`
* configure the config
* start the pipeline with the config
See examples in our wiki's [API-How-To](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/API-How-To#start-streaming-left-and-right-infrared-imagers) and in PR #672.

FYI: You don't need to explicitly cast the pipeline to `std::shared_ptr<rs2_pipeline>` since it can be implicitly casted.

Please try to follow all the above documentation and examples, and if this doesn't answer your question, maybe explain what it is that your are trying to accomplish and we will try to suggest the way

@baptiste-mnh @apoorva2398 Thank you for the pointers. I worked on it a little bit and I made a class that reads bag files using directly the rosbag API. It implements the following functionalities

1. Reads a bag file and outputs a pair of rgb and depth frames asynchronously whenever the function nextFrame is called. The class does not use the realsense pipeline, rather it parses the rosbag messages, so there are no frames dropped

2. Since AFAIK there is no hardware synchronization between rgb and depth streams on the device, the class checks the timestamps and outputs the pair of rgb and depth files that are closest in time (without expensive searches).

3. The depth frame returned is aligned to the camera point of view

4. Depth frame is saved as an xml with the full floating point values in meters for each pixel

4. Together with the frames, the class outputs the frame number and the timestamp for each frame recorded in the rosbag (according to the system clock)

The code should be quite readable, and the dependencies are opencv 3.x, glog, librealsense, and the part of the ros library included as headers, and boost. The files print_helpers.cpp/h are a modified version of the ones included in the rosbag viewer tool example. 

Even if you don't use the class directly, you should be able to copy and paste the methods inside the class to read the rosbag however you want.



[RealSenseHelper.zip](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/files/2270032/RealSenseHelper.zip)



Hope it helps, let me know how you get on.

Thank you for insightful and easy to reproduce feedback. 

There are several issues highlighted by the two snippets:
1. Currently, post-processing filters need to operate on individual frame basis. It would be nice to extend the code to enable users to apply filters on entire framesets (<kbd>enhancement</kbd>) and we are planning to add it.
2. After applying `align`, the `stream_type` of all resulting frames becomes the stream you are aligning to. This, in theory, was to distinguish between real depth frame and a synthetic one (generated from other view-port). An unintended side effect of this design decision is that `get_depth_frame()` can no longer be used to find the depth frame after alignment. We will think how to improve this, as it is very unintuitive. 
3. When single frame (result of post-processing) was being passed to `align` an error was logged, but not thrown, violating projects [error handling policy](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/error_handling.md). This is a (<kbd>bug</kbd>) we should address.

[measure](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/examples/measure) example can also offer some working reference.

The idea is creating a single repository, syncing data into it and then test on either langpacks, syncing, etc, with this "global" repository. It'll really speed up test execution time.

However, there are two ways to implement this idea:
(1) Module call approach: this has already been tried with [this branch](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/compare/master...danuzclaudes:2.9-issue-setupmodule?expand=1).

Several different modules will call `create_sync_global_repo()` in their `setUpModule()` functions and `destroy_global_repo()` in `tearDownModule()`. "A truly global repository would be created just once before each of the tests in `pulp_smash/tests/rpm/cli/test*` modules are executed by the test runner." And then, when the modules are done being executed by the test runner, the global repository can be destroyed. But the question is, will  `python -m unittest2 discover pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli` run all rpm.cli tests concurrently?

(2) Inheritance approach:

Create a `rpm.cli.BaseTestCase`, and each test module is a subclass of it, and calls the method `super.create_sync_global_repo()` which will create its own "global" repository of the module.

Pushed a new approach, which I think fixes it closer to the source of the problem.

The root cause was improperly assuming that every array-like `fit_param` should be split like `X` and `y`. However, only array-likes with the same number of samples as `X` and `y` should be split. Others should just pass through.

Do accomplish this, `CVCache` now knows the length of `X` and `y`. When we unpack a parameter, we split it only if its length matches the length of X and y.
Long term this should be fixed upstream.  Also, I think that we should move the upstream code in dask/array/learn.py to this repository.

I really like the idea of having an ESP-tracking dashboard available. However, I don't think it belongs inside django-anymail itself. I think it could be a separate Django reusable app that would work together with Anymail if both are installed together.

Here's my thinking:

* I really want to avoid storing anything in Anymail. As soon as you introduce models, you also introduce a lot of maintenance complexity... migrations, how to do cleanup (so the log doesn't grow unbounded -- cron jobs? celery tasks?), etc. That's fine--and expected--for an ESP dashboard app, but I wouldn't want to impose it on people who just want django-anymail for sending or receiving through an ESP.

* Just logging the raw data is relatively easy. ([Anymail's signals](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/latest/sending/signals/#post-send-signal) were meant to support this, and there's an example in the docs.) But there's a lot more work in putting together a useful stats dashboard on top of that--and making it perform well with lots of data. That feels beyond the scope of django-anymail.

* All the ESPs that Anymail supports offer already offer some sort of dashboard, with varying degrees of support for the stats you mentioned. (Though all have some limitations. And you can't migrate the historical stats if you're switching ESPs.)

BTW, there was [something similar](https://github.com/brack3t/Djrill/issues/102) discussed over in the Djrill project last year.

If you (or anyone) is interested in starting an ESP-dashboard project like this, I'd be happy to contribute.

> It also would be incapable of detecting duplicate options; currently you get a compilation error if you accidentally specify a holder twice, or give two trampoline classes.

Maybe that's not a big deal.  If you specify multiple trampolines, or multiple holders, you get undefined behaviour (in practice, everything past the first would be ignored).

Regarding the metaclass proposal: Users can now override the default metaclass using `py::metaclass(handle)`.

Renamed `metatype` -> `metaclass` everywhere for consistency. I used the `metatype` name originally since that's what the CPython implementation uses most often, but since `py::metaclass` is sticking around, it makes sense to keep it consistently `metaclass` inside pybind11. I also renamed `internals.metatype` to `internals.default_metaclass` since this can now be overridden by user-defined metaclasses.

> One more that came to me just now is that the differences in this patch also potentially create new opportunities for reference leaks (for instance, are the special static properties collected when a type goes out of scope? This is relevant in cases where types are created dynamically.)

AFAIK there shouldn't be any reference leaks with regard to `static_property` since we don't do any manual `incref`/`decref` -- Python manages everything. `static_property_type` is only ever used in `def_property_static_impl()` (as a borrowed reference) where we only invoke its `__call__` operator. After that, everything is handed off to Python which is going to do the correct reference counting, just like it does for the default `property`.

So bigger picture, the `py::array` and `py::array_t<Type>` (and likewise for most of the python type wrappers pybind provides) are *not* direct storage of arrays; it's probably better to think of them as shared pointers to the underlying python object.  Thus a copy or a move is really just copying or moving the wrapper/shared pointer, not the object itself: the object itself is owned and managed by Python.

Deprecating it seems fine to me, because the confusion seems genuine, but I think that repurposing it in the future to map to Python equality would be similarly confusing.  `py::object` is, in many respects, a holder and C++ holders like `std::shared_ptr` typically do map `==` to pointer equality, not dereferenced value equality.  I think to avoid the confusion going the other way, it might be better to use a named method (e.g. `obj.equals(other)`) for that.  And then perhaps `obj.is(other)` for pointer equality.

I agree that there might not be a clean solution. In some cases (depending on row / column major, etc.), the numpy->Eigen conversion might be possible without copying, in others it might not. I assume that for some use cases, copying is perfectly fine while in others it should be avoided.

Overall I see two options for approaching this:
(A) The Eigen <-> numpy conversion will *never* copy. If the code can't get around a copy, it will throw an exception
(B) The Eigen <-> numpy conversion will try to work without a copy. If this is impossible, the conversion code will create a copy.

Ideally pybind11 would support both options. But I understand that this might be a complicated feature. If a user definitely wants to avoid a copy, they can already go via `py::buffer`. So for a first step, option (A) might be best to improve performance of existing code while being as flexible as possible.

We have to be very careful about how this interacts with people's cert pinning strategies.

In particular, if people achieve their pinning with `--key-path /etc/letsencrypt/archive/example.com/privkey1.pem`, their renewals would later fail if Certbot goes and shreds that file. (It would notice the `key_path` in the renewal configuration file and then try to re-import a copy of the private key that had already been shredded!)

By contrast, @pde mentions that `--key-path /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem` might be safe because it will always point, at a given time, to a copy that still exists and can safely be used.

A possible remedy for this could be to have the `--key-path` parser give a warning or error if the specified path contains `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` (or contains a directory that's identical to it, so that `cd /etc/letsencrypt/archive/example.com; certbot --key-path ./privkey1.pem` would be caught!).

The behavior you're seeing where hooks in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks` only run with the `renew` subcommand is intentional. post_delay can be thought of as a settling time, e.g. after changing a magnetic field you want to wait 1 minute before any further actions are taken. This occurs after each set operation.
inter_delay does not take effect after every set operation, but only if the previous set occured less than this time difference. For example, if you don't want commands to be sent too quickly to the instrument. inter_delay can also be preferred if you don't want a delay to occur every time you set a parameter, but only if it is actually ramping to a value. Does this clarify its use?

The `vals` isn't removed, but moved to the Base parameter, this way it can be used by other Parameter types as well.

As for the wrapping of `get/set_cmd`, they need to be created after calling `super().__init__`, since they depend on attributes initialized there. However, since `get()` and `set()` did not exist when calling `super().__init__`, no wrapping occurred. That's why they need to be explicitly wrapped afterwards.

And `ManualParameter` will stay as a deprecated parameter to help transition to the new Parameter.

import os,time
import uiautomator2 as u2
from multiprocessing import Process,Queue

def get_devices_serials():
    devices_list = []
    fd = os.popen("adb devices")
    devices_list_src = fd.readlines()
    fd.close()
    for device in devices_list_src:
        if "device\n" in device:
            device = device.replace("\tdevice\n","")
            devices_list.append(device)
    return devices_list
    
def worker( serial ):
    os.system("python -m uiautomator2 init  --serial %s"%serial)
    d = u2.connect(serial)
    print(d.info)
    #这里可以添加自己要执行的脚本
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    process_list = []
    serial_list = get_devices_serials()
    for index in range( len(serial_list) ):
        p = Process( target=worker, args=( serial_list[index],  ) )
        p.start()
        process_list.append(p)
    for p in process_list:
        p.join()
    print("all task done!")

Currently, I am implementing a new async plasma client. Here are some ideas:

### C++ Part of Arrow

1. Every client has two socket pools: one for `plasma_manager` and another for `plasma_store`. Each pool has a maximum size.
2. When a client is asked to do a ray task (put, get, transfer, etc)，a C++ coroutine-like object (we call it a `PlasmaCoroutine`) will be created.
3. When a `PlasmaCoroutine` needs a socket to communicate with  `plasma_manager`, a new socket connection will be created and added to the pool. If the pool has met its maximum size, the `PlasmaCoroutine` will be paused.
4. Every client mantains a `Map<int socket_fd, PlasmaCoroutine task>` where `socket_fd` is the currently pending socket of the `PlasmaCoroutine`. All `socket_fd`s are listened by `epoll`.
5.  Every client also mantains a queue for those  `PlasmaCoroutine`s which have not allocated sockets.
6. When a `PlasmaCoroutine` is finished, a `finished` flag will be set. There will also be flags for exceptions. 
7. There will be a `ray_poll` function. When called, it will call `epoll` to get all `socket_fds` that have finished and continue related `PlasmaCoroutine`s. Then `ray_poll` will return finished `PlasmaCoroutine`s' results.

### Python Part of Arrow
1. An asyncio-supported selector based on `ray_poll` will be implemented.
2. Each client will have an asyncio-based event_loop equipped with the selector.
3. All ray tasks will be added to the event_loop so we call poll them.

So then we can have asyncio-friendly async ray tasks.

When a local scheduler fails to schedule an actor task on another local scheduler (in `give_task_to_local_scheduler_retry`), it checks if the destination local scheduler is dead. If yes, it calls `reconstruct_object` on the task's dependencies (possibly only the dummy one) and then caches the task in `cached_submitted_actor_tasks`. Else, it follows the existing codepath to retry the task table message. Exactly one of the submitting local schedulers should succeed in the reconstruction back to the initial creation task.

The one thing I am not sure about is if somehow all reconstruction attempts fail. Then, a bunch of tasks will be cached in `cached_submitted_actor_tasks`, but their dependencies will never be rebuilt, so there will never be a new actor creation notification. I'm still trying to figure out if this could ever happen. If it does, a possible fix would be to call reconstruction on tasks in `cached_submitted_actor_tasks` on a timeout, similar to normal tasks, but I'm worried this might cause even more problems.

`clickhouse-client` works with Native format, not JSON*. But the data for insertion should be specified as list of tuples or list of dicts. All tuples/dicts must be homogeneous by length/keys. It's the only way data can be sent over Native protocol. 

You also can pass generator object as data with insert query. That's the way you can do all required preparations on list of dicts.

To be clear, this illustrates the huge problem with not using --sys-prefix with conda:

1. A user is in environment A. They install widgetsnbextension and then do `jupyter nbextension install --py widgetsnbextension`. This installs the javascript into their home directory, not tied to any environment. They also enable the extension in their home directory.
2. The user switches to a different environment where they have a different ipywidgets and widgetsnbextension installed that is incompatible with the version in (1). Things won't work, since they have the nbextension from the wrong package installed. So they maybe install it again, but that just overwrites the nbextension (if they remember to do something like `--overwrite`), and sets up the same problem for a different environment.

I think it's much better to install to `--sys-prefix`, so the extension and the enabling is constrained to the environment you have activated. You switch to a new environment, and you have a fresh clean slate.

Hi @snowman2, @alfredoahds,

Thanks for the pull request. Before we delve any deeper into this issue, I would like to make sure that I understand the problem you are trying to solve.

Currently, the `datacube.model.Measurement` class isn't actually being used by the ODC code at all. The measurement (or band) information being passed around between the `datacube.Datacube` methods are just plain dictionaries. So your custom index can add the additional information that you want to attach as extra items into those dictionaries and hopefully nothing should break.

My understanding is (please correct me if I am wrong) that you want these extra information to be available as attributes of the loaded `xarray.DataArray`s. In that case what we would need to do [here](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/0e4fe679dd7b72c7cb35ae3e32ce94bcb8b22de8/datacube/api/core.py#L443-L454) is instead of specifying what we think should be copied, we need to specify what we need to filter out (in this case, the `name` and the `dtype`) (and additionally attach the `crs` as an exceptional case).

This is a known problem in python-gitlab, and a complex one to fix in the v3 API. You should probably use explicit pagination to workaround the recursion limit, with the `page` and `per_page` parameters of the `list()` method. Something like:

```python
page = 1
while True:
    next_100 = project.builds.list(per_page=100, page=page)
    if not next_100:
        break
    # store the data or do what you need to do
    page += 1
```
FYI the upcoming v4 support should fix this issue (using generators instead of recursive functions).

A better coding style can be found here https://github.com/tensorlayer/tensorlayer/blob/master/tensorlayer/models/squeezenetv1.py, instead of using `self.all_layers = net.xxxx`, we set the model as `Layer` class.
Therefore, for other models, you can connect tfslim into the layer class.

I think you stumbled upon an actual bug.
I fixed it in 0.17.9, however, I had some problems to upload it on pypi, I will solve within 24h, but for now, you will have to install from source.
